description:
Generate 4 forest tile variants at 1024x1024 using image generation.

Prompt:

Isometric diamond tile for a 2D RTS game. The tile is a perfect diamond (rhombus) shape with a 2:1 width-to-height ratio, viewed from a top-down isometric camera angle (approximately 30 degrees from horizontal). Pixel art style with clean edges. Transparent background outside the diamond. No objects, units, or structures — terrain surface only. The tile should seamlessly connect with identical copies of itself on all four edges.

Dense forest canopy viewed from above. Overlapping tree crowns with dappled light filtering through. Rich foliage texture with depth. Warm sunlight from the upper-left illuminating the canopy tops. Colors: deep greens (#2D5A2D, #1D4A1D, #3D6A3D).

Output: 4 variants at 1024x1024, downscale to 128x64 for final asset. Save to assets/tiles/terrain/forest_*.png
title: Generate terrain tile art: forest

description:
Implement wolf AI behavior as a Gaia fauna unit.

Wolf behavior states (simple state machine):

PATROL (default):
- Wander randomly within 8-tile radius of spawn point
- Pick a random tile in radius, walk to it, idle 3-5 seconds, pick another
- If a lone villager enters aggro_radius (3 tiles): transition to ATTACK
- If 3+ military units enter 5-tile radius: transition to FLEE
- Wolves in the same pack stay loosely grouped (pack cohesion: try to stay within 4 tiles of packmates)

ATTACK:
- Target the nearest villager within aggro radius
- Chase at speed 2.0 (faster than villager at 1.5)
- Attack with 8 damage on contact
- If target dies or moves beyond 6 tiles: return to PATROL
- If 2+ military units approach within 4 tiles: transition to FLEE
- Pack behavior: if one wolf attacks, nearby packmates also aggro the same target

FLEE:
- Run away from the threat (opposite direction) at speed 2.0
- Flee for 5 seconds or until 10 tiles from threat, then return to PATROL
- Flee toward the pack's spawn point (run home)

BEING_FED:
- Entered when a villager uses the Feed command (see domestication story)
- Wolf stops moving, plays eating animation (or idle facing the villager)
- Cannot be aggro'd while being fed
- After feeding completes, return to PATROL (with updated domestication progress)
- If villager is killed during feeding: return to PATROL immediately

DOMESTICATED:
- Terminal state — wolf transforms into Dog unit (see dog story)
- Remove wolf from Gaia faction, create Dog unit for the feeding player

Wolf death:
- When killed: drop 50 Food as a harvestable food source (like hunting a deer)
- Death animation, corpse fades
- Pack members do NOT avenge — they flee when a packmate is killed

Acceptance criteria:
- Wolves patrol naturally around spawn point in loose packs
- Wolves aggro lone villagers and chase them
- Wolves flee from military groups
- Pack behavior: aggro spreads to packmates, flee when packmate dies
- Smooth state transitions with no flickering between states
- Being_fed state correctly pauses aggro
title: Wolf fauna AI: patrol, aggro, and flee behavior

description:
Implement the Dog as a player-owned companion unit created through wolf domestication.

Dog stats:
- HP: 25
- Attack: 5
- Defense: 0
- Speed: 2.5 (fastest non-cavalry land unit)
- LOS: 6 (excellent for scouting)
- Population cost: 0 (does not count toward pop cap)
- Not trainable at any building — only obtained through wolf domestication

Dog behavior AI (semi-autonomous, player can override with move commands):

TOWN_PATROL (default when idle near Town Center):
- If within 10 tiles of a friendly Town Center and no player commands pending
- Wander between buildings in the town (pick random building within 8 tiles, walk to it, idle 2-3s, repeat)
- Provides passive LOS bonus: all buildings within 8 tiles of TC gain +2 LOS
- Bonus stacks up to 2 dogs (max +4 LOS from dog patrol)
- Track which dogs are in town patrol range and recalculate LOS bonus when dogs move in/out

HUNT_ASSIST (when near hunting villagers):
- If within 5 tiles of a villager actively hunting (gathering from deer/boar) and no player commands pending
- Follow the hunting villager at 3-tile distance
- When villager targets prey, dog runs ahead toward the prey
- Hunting gather rate for that villager: +25%
- Deer flee radius reduced from 6 to 3 tiles when dog is within 5 tiles of prey
- Visual: dog runs alongside villager, small bark animation when prey is targeted
- If the villager stops hunting, return to TOWN_PATROL or IDLE

DANGER_ALERT:
- Passive ability, always active regardless of current behavior
- Alert radius: 10 tiles (wider than LOS — the dog senses threats beyond sight)
- When an enemy military unit enters alert radius:
  - Dog plays bark animation + audio cue
  - Minimap ping at dog's location
  - All friendly units within 8 tiles of the dog gain +10% speed for 5 seconds
  - Alert cooldown: 15 seconds (no spam alerts)
- After alerting: transition to FLEE

FLEE:
- Dog runs toward nearest friendly Town Center or military unit at full speed (2.5)
- Dogs NEVER fight — they always flee from enemy units
- If cornered (no path to safety): cower in place

FOLLOW (player command):
- Player right-clicks a villager or unit: dog follows that unit at 3-tile distance
- Useful for scouting or manually assigning to a hunting party
- Override behavior until new command or unit dies

GARRISON:
- Dogs can be garrisoned inside Town Center for protection during raids
- Garrisoned dogs still contribute to town LOS bonus (they're still in the town)
- Ungarrison to resume patrol

description:
In the Stone and Bronze Ages, players can domesticate wolves into dogs by feeding them. Dogs provide hunting, scouting, and early warning benefits. This is one of the first meaningful decisions a player makes — spending scarce early food on a wolf is a gamble that pays off over time.

DECISION

1. WOLVES AS GAIA FAUNA
Wolves spawn during map generation as neutral (Gaia-owned) fauna, similar to deer. They roam in small packs of 2-3 near forests and away from starting positions.
- Spawn count: 3-5 wolf packs per map (6-15 individual wolves)
- Behavior: wolves patrol a small territory (8-tile radius from spawn), wander randomly
- Wolves are HOSTILE to villagers who get too close (aggro radius: 3 tiles). They attack with 8 damage, 30 HP, speed 2.0 (faster than villagers). A lone villager will die to a wolf pack.
- Wolves flee from groups of 3+ military units
- Wolves can be killed for 50 Food (huntable like deer, but they fight back)

So the player has a choice: HUNT the wolves for quick food, or FEED them to domesticate.

2. FEEDING / DOMESTICATION PROCESS
To domesticate a wolf, a villager must carry food to it using a new "Feed" command:
(a) Select villager, right-click wolf while holding a modifier key (Shift+right-click) or use a "Feed" button in the command panel
(b) Villager walks toward the wolf carrying food from the nearest stockpile (costs 25 Food per feeding)
(c) Villager plays a "feeding" animation at 2-tile distance (doesn't get close enough to trigger aggro)
(d) Wolf enters a "being fed" state — stops patrolling, plays eating animation
(e) After feeding, the wolf's domestication progress increases by 33%
(f) Three feedings (75 Food total) fully domesticates the wolf → it transforms into a Dog unit owned by the player
(g) Domestication progress decays slowly if not fed within 60 seconds (10% per minute) — you need to commit, not just feed once and forget
(h) If another player feeds the same wolf, progress resets to that player (contested domestication)

3. DOG UNIT
Once domesticated, the wolf becomes a Dog — a non-trainable companion unit:
- HP: 25 (fragile, not a combat unit)
- Attack: 5 (light, can help finish off wounded units)
- Defense: 0
- Speed: 2.5 (very fast — faster than all land units except cavalry)
- LOS: 6 (excellent scouting range, higher than any Stone Age unit)
- Population cost: 0 (dogs don't count toward pop cap — they're companions, not trained soldiers)
- Cannot be produced at any building — only obtained through domestication
- Lifespan: unlimited (they don't die of old age)

4. DOG BEHAVIORS AND BONUSES

(a) HUNTING ASSIST
Dogs automatically follow villagers who are hunting (gathering from deer/boar). When a dog is within 5 tiles of a hunting villager:
- Hunting gather rate increased by 25% (dogs help chase and corner prey)
- Deer do not flee as far when a dog is nearby (reduced flee radius from 6 tiles to 3)
- Visual: dog runs alongside the villager during hunting, barks at prey

(b) DANGER ALERT
Dogs detect approaching enemy units earlier than other units:
- Dog alert radius: 10 tiles (wider than its LOS of 6 — the dog "senses" threats it can't see)
- When an enemy unit enters the alert radius, the dog barks (audio cue + visual bark icon)
- A minimap ping appears at the dog's location
- All friendly units within 8 tiles of the alerting dog receive a brief speed boost (+10% for 5 seconds) — adrenaline response to the warning
- Alert triggers once per enemy group approach, not per-frame (cooldown: 15 seconds)

(c) TOWN VISIBILITY BONUS
When at least one dog is within 10 tiles of a Town Center:
- All buildings within 8 tiles of the Town Center gain +2 LOS (dogs patrol the settlement, extending sight)
- This bonus stacks up to 2 dogs (max +4 LOS from dogs)
- Visual: dogs occasionally wander between buildings in the town (patrol animation)
- The bonus is passive — no micromanagement needed, just keep dogs alive near your TC

(d) DOG BEHAVIOR AI
Dogs are semi-autonomous — the player can issue move commands, but dogs also have AI:
- If idle near Town Center: wander between buildings (town patrol)
- If idle near hunting villagers: follow and assist
- If enemy detected: bark alert, then flee toward nearest friendly military unit or Town Center
- Dogs never attack-move or stand ground — they always flee from combat
- Can be garrisoned inside Town Center for protection during raids

5. LIMITATIONS AND COUNTERPLAY
- Dogs are fragile (25 HP) — a few archer shots kill them. Protecting dogs during raids matters.
- 75 Food in Stone Age is expensive (roughly 2 villagers worth). Domesticating multiple wolves is a significant investment.
- Only 3-5 packs on the map means dogs are a LIMITED RESOURCE — competitive scouting to find and feed wolves before opponents
- Wolves near the center of the map are contested — feeding them is risky if enemies are nearby
- Dogs become less impactful in later ages as military units have higher LOS and better scouting. They remain useful for hunting bonus and alert.
- If all dogs die, the player has no way to get more (unless the map has undomesticated wolves remaining)

6. CIV INTERACTIONS
- Mesopotamia: no specific wolf/dog bonus
- Rome: potential future unique tech "War Dogs" — dogs gain +10 HP and +3 attack, become light combat units
- Polynesia: no specific wolf/dog bonus (dogs historically important in Polynesian culture — could add a bonus later)

7. AGE RESTRICTIONS
- Wolf feeding is available from Stone Age (the very start of the game)
- Dogs remain useful through all ages but have diminishing relative value
- No tech required to feed wolves — it's a discovered mechanic (tooltip on wolf hover explains it)

CONSEQUENCES
- Creates an interesting Stone Age decision: spend 75 food on a wolf or train another villager?
- Rewards map exploration (finding wolf packs before opponents)
- Adds life to the early game — wolves roaming, dogs patrolling towns
- Hunting becomes more engaging with dog assist
- Early warning system rewards players who invest in dogs
- Dogs become an emotional attachment for players (protecting them matters)
- Limited supply creates scarcity and competition
- Simple mechanic with deep strategic implications
title: ADR-010: Wolf Domestication — Early Game Companion Mechanic

description:
Defines the visual production pipeline for all game assets: tiles, units, buildings, UI elements, effects. This is the single highest-risk bottleneck for a solo/small team building an isometric RTS.

DECISION

1. Tile geometry: 128x64px diamond tiles (2:1 ratio). Elevation step: 32px vertical offset per level (half tile height). 3 usable elevation levels.

2. Sprite scale contract:
   - Villager: ~48px tall (standing), fits within one tile footprint
   - Infantry/military: ~52-56px tall
   - Cavalry/mounted: ~64px tall
   - Buildings (1x1): 128x128px max
   - Buildings (2x2): 256x192px max
   - Buildings (3x3): 384x256px max
   - Trees/resources: ~64-80px tall, single tile footprint

3. Animation budget per unit type (AUTHORING cost, not output count):
   - Idle: 4 keyframes (rendered to 32 output frames via 8 cameras)
   - Walk: 8 keyframes (rendered to 64 output frames)
   - Attack: 6 keyframes (rendered to 48 output frames)
   - Death: 6 keyframes (rendered to 6 output frames, 1 direction mirrored)
   - Gather (villager, per resource): 6 keyframes x 4 types (rendered to 96 output frames each)
   - Military unit authoring cost: ~24 keyframes -> ~150 output frames (6x multiplication via render rig)
   - Villager authoring cost: ~48 keyframes -> ~230 output frames
   The 3D render pipeline eliminates per-direction manual work entirely.

4. Art production method: THREE-PHASE AUTOMATED PIPELINE.

   Phase 1 (prototype) — FULLY PROCEDURAL, ZERO MANUAL ART:
   A Python/GDScript generator creates all placeholder sprites programmatically.
   Colored geometric shapes: diamonds for tiles, circles for units, rectangles for buildings.
   Script generates all directions, animation frames, player color variants.
   Output committed to repo OR generated at build time.
   Game is playable within hours of project creation.

   Phase 2 (alpha) — BLENDER 3D-TO-2D RENDER PIPELINE:
   This is the core production method, modeled after how Age of Empires was actually made.
   (a) Model unit/building once in Blender (low-poly, stylized)
   (b) Rig with standard humanoid skeleton (units) or no rig (buildings)
   (c) Animate once — walk cycle, attack, idle, death. ONE direction only.
   (d) Isometric render rig: 8 cameras at 45-degree intervals, all at iso angle (30 deg from horizontal), orthographic projection, consistent lighting
   (e) Blender script batch-renders all animations from all 8 cameras automatically
   (f) Pipeline script packs rendered PNGs into spritesheets, generates Godot SpriteFrames resources
   (g) Player color variants: render with neutral base, apply team color via shader at runtime (NOT re-rendering per player)
   One animation authored = 8 directions output. One model = all player colors via shader.

   Phase 3 (polish) — TEXTURE REFINEMENT:
   AI-generated textures applied to 3D models, re-rendered through same pipeline.
   Hand-touch-up on final spritesheets in Aseprite for hero units and unique buildings.
   Pipeline stays the same — only the model/texture quality improves.

5. Player color system: Runtime shader recoloring, NOT baked sprites.
   Units and buildings rendered with a designated "team color" mask region.
   A Godot shader swaps the mask color to the player's color at runtime.
   This means ONE set of sprites serves all players — no per-player sprite variants needed.
   Player colors: Blue (#2E86DE), Red (#E74C3C), Teal (#1ABC9C), Orange (#F39C12).

6. Color palettes per age cluster:
   - Stone/Bronze: earthy browns, muted greens, terracotta
   - Iron/Medieval: deeper greens, grays, stone blues
   - Industrial: brick reds, iron grays, smoke, amber
   - Information/Singularity: steel blues, white, neon accents, holographic

7. Tileset production:
   - 5 base terrains, ~15-20 variants each via Godot TileMap terrain autotiling
   - Terrain tiles: procedurally generated for Phase 1, Blender-rendered for Phase 2
   - Total: ~120 tiles minimum viable (80-100 terrain + 20 mountain + 12 elevation)

8. Asset file organization:
   - assets/sprites/units/{unit_name}/ — spritesheet per unit (shared across players via shader)
   - assets/sprites/buildings/{building_name}/ — spritesheet per building
   - assets/tiles/terrain/ — tileset textures
   - assets/ui/ — UI elements, icons, fonts
   - assets/effects/ — particle textures, projectiles
   - blender/ — source .blend files, render rig template, batch export scripts
   - tools/ — procedural generator, spritesheet packer, CI validation scripts

9. Automation toolchain (all scripted, all CI-validated):
   - tools/generate_placeholders.py — generates Phase 1 geometric sprites
   - tools/spritesheet_packer.py — packs rendered PNGs into spritesheets + Godot resources
   - blender/render_rig.blend — template scene with 8 cameras, lighting, render settings
   - blender/batch_render.py — Blender script: render all animations for a model from all cameras
   - CI step: validate sprite dimensions match scale contract, frame counts match budget

CONSEQUENCES

- Phase 1 is FULLY AUTOMATED — zero manual art, game playable immediately
- Phase 2 authoring cost: ~24 keyframes per military unit (not 150 hand-drawn frames)
- 3D pipeline gives 6-8x frame multiplication — 8 directions for the cost of 1
- Player colors handled by shader — no per-player sprite duplication
- Same pipeline serves all 3 phases — only input quality improves
- Blender is free, scriptable, and has excellent isometric rendering support
- CI validates sprite consistency — wrong dimensions caught before merge
- Total authoring cost for 6 unit types: ~200 keyframes (not 1050 hand-drawn frames)
title: ADR-008: Art Pipeline — Isometric Asset Production Strategy

description:
Generate 4 dirt tile variants at 1024x1024 using image generation.

Prompt:

Isometric diamond tile for a 2D RTS game. The tile is a perfect diamond (rhombus) shape with a 2:1 width-to-height ratio, viewed from a top-down isometric camera angle (approximately 30 degrees from horizontal). Pixel art style with clean edges. Transparent background outside the diamond. No objects, units, or structures — terrain surface only. The tile should seamlessly connect with identical copies of itself on all four edges.

Packed earth with subtle cracks and pebbles. Dry, well-trodden ground with slight color variation suggesting foot traffic. Warm sunlight from the upper-left. Colors: warm browns (#8B7355, #A0845C, #6B5B42).

Output: 4 variants at 1024x1024, downscale to 128x64 for final asset. Save to assets/tiles/terrain/dirt_*.png

description:

- [ ] Build order templates loaded from data/ai/build_orders.json per difficulty

- [ ] AI advances ages when prerequisites met and resources available
- [ ] AI builds houses proactively to avoid population cap stalls
- [ ] Economic decisions run on configurable tick interval (not every frame)
- [ ] All economic thresholds and ratios data-driven from JSON
- [ ] Unit tests for villager allocation logic
- [ ] Integration test: AI reaches Bronze Age within expected time range

description:
AI builds military units, forms armies, and decides when/where to attack.

Acceptance Criteria:

- [ ] Army threshold before attacking loaded from JSON per difficulty
- [ ] AI targets weakest enemy expansion or undefended resource lines
- [ ] AI retreats damaged units below configurable HP threshold
- [ ] AI garrisons units in buildings when outnumbered
- [ ] Attack timing varies by difficulty (Easy: late, Expert: aggressive timing attacks)
- [ ] All army thresholds, composition ratios data-driven from JSON
- [ ] Unit tests for target prioritization logic

description: AI researches technologies and advances ages. Economic AI prioritizes age advancement. Aggressive AI prioritizes military upgrades. AI follows a predefined tech order per personality per age.

description:
Four difficulty tiers controlling AI economic bonus, reaction speed, and strategic sophistication.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Difficulty enum in data/settings/ai_difficulty.json with 4 tiers
- [ ] Easy: 0.7x gather rate, 2s reaction delay, no rush strategies
- [ ] Normal: 1.0x gather rate, 1s reaction delay, basic build orders
- [ ] Hard: 1.2x gather rate, 0.5s reaction delay, adaptive strategies
- [ ] Expert: 1.5x gather rate, 0s reaction delay, full strategic AI

- [ ] Difficulty selection in game setup screen
- [ ] Tests cover each tier's parameter loading
title: AI difficulty tiers — Easy, Normal, Hard, Expert

description:
Adjust AI behavior to account for the Knowledge Burning mechanic.

AI defensive adjustments:
- AI assigns a "tech vulnerability score" to each of its Town Centers based on:
  - Number of researched techs (more techs = more to lose = higher priority to defend)
  - Proximity to enemy units/bases (closer = more vulnerable)
  - Current military presence near the TC (more defenders = less vulnerable)
- AI allocates more military units to defend TCs with high vulnerability scores
- The defensive allocation increases in later ages (more techs at stake)
- Aggressive AI personality still raids more, but maintains a minimum TC garrison proportional to tech count

AI offensive adjustments:
- AI recognizes enemy TCs as high-value targets (not just for territory, but for tech regression)
- When AI has military advantage, it may prioritize TC snipes over unit kills
- If the enemy is ahead in tech, AI increases priority of TC raids as a catch-up mechanism
- Aggressive AI explicitly targets tech-leading opponents' TCs

AI forward TC decision:
- AI evaluates risk/reward of building forward TCs:
  - Benefit: map control, shorter unit reinforcement path
  - Risk: if destroyed, lose a tech
  - Decision weight: build forward TCs only when military advantage is clear, or when the forward position has natural defenses (hills, river, chokepoint)
- Economic AI personality avoids forward TCs (risk-averse)
- Aggressive AI personality builds forward TCs more freely

AI response to own tech loss:
- When AI loses a tech from TC destruction:
  - Increase overall military production priority for 2 minutes (defensive reaction)
  - Re-research the lost tech as soon as resources allow (high priority)
  - If the lost TC was forward: do not rebuild it in the same exposed location

Acceptance criteria:
- AI garrisons TCs proportional to tech count
- AI targets enemy TCs when ahead militarily
- AI avoids building forward TCs when tech regression risk is high
- AI re-researches lost techs promptly
- Different AI personalities (aggressive/economic/balanced) handle the mechanic differently
- AI does not over-prioritize TC defense to the point of never attacking
title: AI: factor tech regression risk into Town Center defense priority

description:
AI understands the Singularity victory condition and reacts to enemy progress toward it.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] AI monitors enemy tech research alerts (Singularity techs are public)
- [ ] AI increases military aggression when enemy researches Singularity-tier techs
- [ ] AI pursues Singularity victory path on Hard/Expert if ahead in tech
- [ ] AI prioritizes destroying enemy GPU Foundry / Transformer Lab / AGI Core
- [ ] Response urgency scales with enemy progress through the Singularity chain
- [ ] All thresholds and priorities data-driven from JSON
- [ ] Unit tests for Singularity threat assessment logic
title: AI Singularity awareness — race and disruption strategy

description: Cluster screen-space offsets on resource nodes can map to adjacent water tiles when converted back to grid coords via IsoUtils.screen_to_grid(). This causes move_to() to silently reject the move, leaving villagers stuck in MOVING_TO_RESOURCE state. Fix by storing the logical grid_position on resource nodes and using it for pathfinding.
title: Fix villagers disappearing when gathering trees on water-adjacent tiles

description: Villager sprite Sprite2D child was created with z_index=-1, putting it below terrain tiles which have z_index=col+row. Change to z_index=0 so sprites render at the same level as the unit's _draw() output.

Debug server v3: improved observability and game control

description: Add cmd_units() to tools/ror that calls GET /entities?category=own_unit and prints a compact table: NAME | ACTION | CARRIED | HP | POSITION. Support --all flag to include enemy units and wild fauna. No server changes needed — uses existing entities endpoint with entity_category inference from PR #221.

description:

Only sets resources that are specified (omitted resources unchanged). Uses ResourceManager.set_resource() or equivalent.
Essential for integration test scenarios that need specific starting conditions without waiting for gather cycles.
Add corresponding 'ror game-cmd set-resources food=500 wood=300' CLI usage.

- {"action": "load", "slot": "test"} — triggers SaveManager to load from saved state

description: Address code duplication, performance bottlenecks, and architectural issues identified in comprehensive codebase review. Covers shared utility extraction, spatial indexing, god object decomposition, event-driven rendering, and organizational cleanup.
title: Epic: Codebase Health — Duplication, Performance & Architecture

description: Extract _get_game_delta() (duplicated in 8 files: wolf_ai, pirate_ai, dog_ai, historical_event_manager, pirate_manager, trade_cart_ai, prototype_resource_node, pandemic_manager) and _dl_settings()/_load_config() (duplicated in 40+ files) into a shared utility module. Create a GameUtils or NodeUtils autoload/static class. Eliminates ~200+ lines of copy-paste code.
title: Extract shared utilities for _get_game_delta() and _dl_settings()

description: Three UI duplication issues: (1) pause_menu.gd _refresh_save_slots() and _refresh_load_slots() are 95% identical — consolidate into _refresh_slot_buttons(buttons, allow_disable). (2) game_lobby_screen.gd and civ_selection_screen.gd both implement _build_civ_card() with minor sizing differences — extract to shared CivCardBuilder. (3) postgame_stats_screen.gd has 4 near-identical time series builders — extract _build_time_series_for_field() generic method.
title: Consolidate save/load slot refresh and civ card duplication

description: prototype_building.gd has _draw_progress_bar() and _draw_hp_bar() (lines ~285-314) with near-identical background/fill/border pattern. Consolidate into single _draw_bar(fill_ratio, fill_color) method. Check if prototype_unit.gd has similar bar drawing that can share the same utility.
title: Consolidate progress bar and HP bar drawing

description: prototype_unit.gd calls queue_redraw() every frame while moving (line ~230), and also in _tick_heal, _tick_combat, _try_retaliate, and other state updates — even when nothing visually changed. With 100+ units, this creates 100+ redundant draw calls per frame. Add a _visual_dirty flag: only set it when facing direction changes, HP changes, gather state changes, or combat state changes. Only call queue_redraw() when the flag is set. Note: may be partially addressed by the prototype_unit decomposition story.
title: Optimize unit queue_redraw() calls with dirty flag

description: resource_bar.gd _process() calls _update_transit_labels() every frame, querying river_transport.get_in_transit_resources() and iterating 5 resource types. cursor_overlay.gd updates position every frame even when mouse hasn't moved. Replace with signal-driven updates: resource bar listens for transit_changed signal; cursor overlay only updates on InputEventMouseMotion.
title: Replace polling with signals in resource bar and cursor overlay

description: fog_of_war_layer.gd update_fog() iterates previous visible tiles and calls set_cell() for each changed tile individually. Each set_cell() is O(log n) for the tilemap internal structure. With 100+ tiles changing per visibility update, this accumulates. Batch the updates or defer to next frame to avoid frame spikes.
title: Batch fog of war tilemap updates

description: prototype_unit.gd _deal_damage_to_target() creates two new dictionaries (_build_stats_dict and _build_target_stats_dict) on every attack (every 0.5-2s per unit). With 20+ active combats, this creates GC pressure. Reuse dictionaries with clear() + reset, or pass pre-allocated stat references instead of creating new dicts each time.
title: Reduce combat stats dictionary allocations

description: prototype_input.gd _assign_slots() is O(n²): for each of n selected units, it iterates all n slot positions to find minimum distance. With 40 selected units = 1,600 distance comparisons per move order. Also, pathfinding_grid.gd get_formation_targets() uses an array with O(n) membership check in a spiral search. Use a Set for O(1) membership check and consider Hungarian algorithm or sorted greedy approach for slot assignment.
title: Optimize formation slot assignment from O(n²) to O(n log n)

description: historical_event_manager.gd (681 lines) uses 5+ parallel dictionaries for per-player plague state: _plague_mit_death, _plague_mit_duration, _aftermath_active, _aftermath_timer, _renaissance_triggered, _renaissance_active. All mutated in _process(). Replace with a PlagueState class with proper member variables. Also extract event-specific logic into separate classes (BlackPlagueEvent, RenaissanceEvent) to reduce the file's 681-line size.
title: Replace parallel dictionaries with state objects in historical_event_manager

description: prototype_input.gd (612 lines) _issue_context_command() has 10+ if/elif branches for different command types (feed, build, gather, garrison, embark, etc). Extract a CommandHandler interface with pluggable handlers for each command type. This reduces prototype_input.gd's size and makes it easier to add new commands without modifying the central dispatcher.
title: Extract command handlers from prototype_input.gd

description: data/settings/ has 37 files in a flat directory. Organize into subdirectories: gameplay/ (combat, gathering, construction, population, etc), ai/ (ai_personalities, ai_build_orders, etc), mechanics/ (trade, corruption, pandemic, etc), graphics/ (rendering, particles). Update DataLoader to handle subdirectory paths. All references in scripts that load settings will need path updates.
title: Reorganize data/settings/ into subdirectories

description: Replace O(n²) scene tree scanning with spatial partitioning. Current hot paths: prototype_unit._scan_for_targets() iterates ALL children per unit on 0.5s timer; prototype_unit._find_nearest_drop_off() and _try_find_replacement_resource() scan all children; target_detector.detect() iterates all registered entities; ai_military._refresh_entity_lists() re-classifies all children every 3s. Implement a grid-based spatial index or maintain faction-indexed unit/building registries updated via signals on spawn/death.
title: Add spatial indexing for unit/building lookups

description: scripts/buildings/, scripts/resources/, scripts/units/, scripts/combat/ exist with .gdkeep files but are empty — all logic lives in scripts/prototype/. Either: (a) begin migrating relevant code from prototype/ into these directories as part of the decomposition stories, or (b) remove the empty directories if the prototype/ flat structure is intentional. Decision should align with the prototype_unit and prototype_main decomposition stories.
title: Clean up empty placeholder directories in scripts/

description: 13 of 15 buildings are missing garrison_capacity field. Some buildings have inconsistent optional fields (dock, market, factory, agi_core, wonder missing expected fields). Ensure all building JSON files have a uniform set of fields (even if null/0 for unused ones). Validate against the building schema in data/schemas/. Add schema validation to CI if not already present.
title: Standardize building JSON data with consistent optional fields

description: ai_military.gd (739 lines) handles unit recruitment, attack planning, tactical positioning, and resource allocation all in one file. ai_economy.gd (661 lines) similarly handles building, unit production, resource management, and villager assignment. Split ai_military into AIMilitaryStrategy (what to build, when to attack) and AIMilitaryTactics (how to position/move units, target prioritization). Split ai_economy into AIBuildPlanner and AIResourceAllocator.
title: Decompose AI monoliths into strategy/tactics layers

description: In prototype_main._start_game(), civ bonuses are applied in _setup_civilizations() before ResourceManager.init_player(0) is called (which happens later in _setup_hud() → _setup_resource_bar()). This works currently by coincidence but is fragile — reordering setup steps could cause bonuses to be applied to uninitialized resources. Make the dependency explicit: either initialize resources before applying bonuses, or document the ordering constraint with an assertion.
title: Harden initialization order between civ bonuses and resource manager

description: visibility_manager.gd update_visibility() runs at ~5Hz and for each player unit (30+), computes shadowcasting FOV — creating new dictionaries and merging them repeatedly. Optimizations: (1) cache FOV shapes per unit type (units of same type at same position have identical FOV), (2) only recompute for units that moved since last update, (3) consider simpler circular FOV for most units, reserving shadowcasting for buildings/towers.
title: Optimize visibility manager FOV computation

description: prototype_building.gd _process() ticks garrison arrow logic for every building (20+) every frame, even buildings with no garrisoned units. Add an early-out check: skip arrow tick if garrison is empty. Consider using Timer nodes instead of manual delta accumulation for buildings that do have garrison.
title: Only tick garrison arrow logic for buildings with garrisoned units

description: minimap.gd calls queue_redraw() unconditionally in _process() every frame, triggering ~80,000 set_pixel operations at 60fps (two 200x200 images). Replace with a dirty flag that only triggers redraw when visibility or fog state actually changes. Connect to visibility_manager signals instead of polling.
title: Make minimap redraw event-driven with dirty flag

description: prototype_unit.gd is 1,196 lines with 109 member variables, 83 functions, and 5+ interleaved state machines. Exceeds the project's 1000-line limit. Extract into composition pattern: GathererComponent (gather states, drop-off finding, resource targeting), CombatantComponent (combat states, target scanning, damage dealing, pursuit), TransportComponent (embark/disembark, passenger management). Keep unit as coordinator. This also fixes the excessive queue_redraw() calls — only redraw when component state actually changes visual appearance.
title: Decompose prototype_unit.gd into components

description: wolf_ai.gd, pirate_ai.gd, and dog_ai.gd share identical implementations of: _tick_movement() (lines ~520-530 in each), _get_game_delta(), _dl_settings()/_load_config(), and near-identical target scanning (_scan_for_aggro_target / _scan_for_target / _find_hunting_villager). Create a BaseFaunaAI class with shared movement, config loading, and generic target scanning (taking filter callback). Each subclass only implements behavior-specific logic.
title: Extract BaseFaunaAI from wolf/pirate/dog AI

description: prototype_unit.gd _get_autoload() calls Engine.get_main_loop().root.get_node_or_null() with string lookup 6+ times per frame per unit. With 100 units, that's 600+ redundant lookups per frame. Cache GameManager, ResourceManager, DataLoader, CivBonusManager references in _ready() or on first access. Apply same pattern to all files using _get_autoload() or _dl_settings() (40+ files, but most will be fixed by the shared utility extraction story).
title: Cache autoload references in _ready() instead of per-frame lookups

description: prototype_main.gd is 1,199 lines — a god object with 64 preloaded scripts, 28 manager references, ~150 lines of setup code. Handles scene assembly, game state transitions, all child system setup, pause/resume, input interception, and victory/defeat flow. Extract: GameBootstrapper (one-time setup, manager initialization, scene assembly), GameFlowController (civ selection → game start → pause → end state machine). Push manager setup into factory pattern. Reduces coupling and makes the game flow testable.
title: Split prototype_main.gd into bootstrapper and flow controller

description: Multiple systems scan get_children() to find units by owner_id and type: ai_military._refresh_entity_lists() every 3s, ai_economy similarly, prototype_unit._scan_for_targets(). Create a UnitRegistry (or EntityRegistry) that maintains indexed lists by faction and unit_category. Register on spawn, unregister on death/free. Provides O(1) faction lookup instead of O(n) tree scanning. Works alongside spatial indexing story for complete solution.
title: Implement faction/type unit registries to replace scene tree scanning

description: Currently prototype_main.gd has hard references to 28 systems, and ResourceManager.add_resource() is called directly by units, buildings, tech, trade, and civs. Changes to shared interfaces ripple across 40+ files. Bidirectional coupling exists between TechManager↔HistoricalEventManager and PopulationManager↔PandemicManager. Introduce an EventBus singleton to decouple systems: resource changes, combat events, tech completions, and victory conditions flow through the bus. Add event logging for debugging. This also provides a natural point for event ordering guarantees (currently signal handler execution order is undefined).
title: Introduce event bus for cross-system communication

description:
Two early-game friction mechanics: corruption (persistent scaling drain on resources with empire size) and pandemics (random events reducing productivity and killing villagers). Both mitigated and eventually eliminated by specific techs. All settings data-driven from JSON.

Key mechanics:
- Corruption: active ages 1-4, scales with building count above 8, caps at 30%, countered by Code of Laws/Banking/Civil Service
- Pandemics: active ages 0-3, random check every 2 min, countered by Herbalism/Sanitation/Vaccines
- Settings in data/settings/corruption.json and data/settings/pandemics.json
title: Epic: Pandemic & Corruption Event System

description:
Implement corruption mechanic that reduces resource income based on building count. Active ages 1-4. Starts above 8 buildings at 1.5%/building, caps at 30%. Affected: food, wood, stone, gold (not Knowledge). Countered by Code of Laws (-30%), Banking (-25%), Civil Service (eliminates). Settings in data/settings/corruption.json.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Corruption rate calculated per tick based on building count and threshold
- [ ] Rate capped at max_corruption (30%)
- [ ] Tech reductions applied cumulatively
- [ ] Civil Service sets corruption to 0 permanently
- [ ] Knowledge resource immune to corruption
- [ ] Only active in configured ages
- [ ] UI indicator shows current corruption rate
- [ ] Unit tests for rate calculation with various building counts
- [ ] Unit tests for tech reduction stacking
title: Corruption system: resource drain scaling with empire size

description:
Implement pandemic random events based on population density. Active ages 0-3. Checked every 2 min, base 5% chance (+2% per villager above 15). Effects: -30% work rate, 5% villager death chance, 45s duration. Mitigated by Herbalism (-25%), Sanitation (-50%), Vaccines (immunity). Visual: green particle cloud. Audio: plague bell toll. Settings in data/settings/pandemics.json.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Timer-based check every 120 game seconds
- [ ] Probability scales with population above density threshold
- [ ] Work rate penalty applied to all villagers during pandemic
- [ ] Death chance rolled per villager per pandemic tick
- [ ] Tech mitigations reduce severity and probability
- [ ] Vaccines grant full immunity
- [ ] Green particle cloud VFX over Town Center during pandemic
- [ ] Plague bell toll audio on pandemic start
- [ ] Only active in configured ages
- [ ] Unit tests for probability calculation
- [ ] Unit tests for mitigation stacking
title: Pandemic event system: random disease outbreaks

description:
Implement named historical events that fire based on age/tech milestones. Black Plague is a guaranteed mega-pandemic in Medieval Age (-50% work rate, 15% death chance, 90s). Renaissance is a per-player golden age triggered by Printing Press + Banking + Guilds (+35% research, +50% Knowledge, 3 min). Phoenix interaction: Renaissance within 120s of plague gives 1.5x bonuses. Settings in data/settings/historical_events.json.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Black Plague fires once per game in Medieval Age (random delay 180-360s into age)
- [ ] Black Plague affects all players simultaneously
- [ ] Black Plague effects: -50% work rate, 15% villager death chance, -75% trade income, 90s duration
- [ ] Medical tech mitigations reduce Black Plague severity (Herbalism, Sanitation, Vaccines=immune)
- [ ] Post-plague aftermath bonuses: +15% work rate, +20% research speed for 120s
- [ ] Renaissance triggers per-player when Printing Press + Banking + Guilds all researched
- [ ] Renaissance effects: +35% research, +50% Knowledge gen, +20% gold, 180s duration
- [ ] Bonus effects for 3+ Libraries and 2+ Markets during Renaissance
- [ ] Phoenix interaction: Renaissance within 120s of plague survival → 1.5x multiplier
- [ ] Visual effects: dark fog (plague), golden glow (renaissance)
- [ ] Audio: death knells (plague), fanfare (renaissance)
- [ ] Announcement text for both events
- [ ] Unit tests for trigger conditions, effect application, mitigation stacking
- [ ] Integration test: advance to Medieval → plague fires → research 3 techs → renaissance triggers
title: Historical events system: Black Plague and Renaissance

description:
When an enemy army destroys a Town Center, the defending civilization loses its most recently researched technology. This represents the burning of libraries, the scattering of scholars, and the destruction of institutional knowledge that historically accompanied the sack of cities.

This mechanic creates high stakes for base defense, meaningful punishment for over-expansion, and dramatic comeback moments when an attacker cripples a tech leader.

DECISION

1. TRIGGER CONDITION: TOWN CENTER DESTRUCTION
Tech regression triggers ONLY when a Town Center is destroyed by an enemy. Specifically:
- The destroyed TC must have been fully constructed (not a foundation)
- The destroyer must be an enemy player (not self-destruction or Gaia)
- Triggers EVERY time a TC is destroyed, not just the last one
- If a player has multiple TCs and loses one, they still lose a tech
- Rationale: each TC represents a center of learning. Losing ANY city is catastrophic to knowledge.

This makes forward Town Centers (aggressive expansions) risky — they're not just map control, they're tech insurance liabilities.

2. WHICH TECH IS LOST
The most recently researched technology is reverted. Rules:
(a) The tech research history is tracked as an ordered list (first researched → most recent)
(b) On TC destruction, pop the most recent tech from the list — it becomes un-researched
(c) Age advancements CANNOT be lost — only individual techs within ages. Losing a tech doesn't de-age you. Rationale: once a civilization has undergone an age transition, the foundational knowledge is too distributed to be lost from one city burning.
(d) If the player has no researched techs (only age advancements), no tech is lost
(e) Unique civ techs CAN be lost (they're regular techs, just civ-specific)
(f) The Singularity chain techs (Transformer Architecture, etc.) CAN be lost — this is intentionally devastating. It means defending your base in the late game is critical for the Singularity victory path.

3. EFFECTS OF LOSING A TECH
When a tech is reverted:
(a) The bonus from that tech is immediately removed from all units/buildings/economy
(b) Unit stat upgrades revert: if you lose "+2 Infantry Attack", all infantry immediately lose 2 attack
(c) Economic bonuses revert: if you lose "+15% farm output", farms immediately produce less
(d) Building unlocks: if the lost tech unlocked a building type, existing buildings of that type REMAIN but you cannot build new ones until re-researched
(e) Unit unlocks: if the lost tech unlocked a unit type, existing units REMAIN but you cannot produce new ones until re-researched
(f) In-progress research at other buildings is NOT affected (you don't lose partially-researched techs)

4. RE-RESEARCHING LOST TECH
Lost techs can be re-researched:
- Full resource cost (no discount — the knowledge is truly lost)
- Requires the same prerequisites as original research (building + age)
- Can be researched at any surviving building of the appropriate type
- The tech is added back to the end of the research history (it becomes the "most recent" again)

5. KNOWLEDGE BURNING VISUAL AND NOTIFICATION
When a TC is destroyed and tech is lost:
(a) Dramatic visual: a "burning scroll" particle effect rises from the destroyed TC
(b) Screen flash for the affected player (brief amber/red overlay)
(c) Large notification: "City destroyed! Lost technology: [Tech Name]"
(d) For the attacking player: "Enemy city sacked! They lost: [Tech Name]"
(e) Game-wide notification (all players): "[Player] lost knowledge of [Tech Name]"
(f) The tech tree UI shows the lost tech reverting (un-highlighting animation)
(g) Audio: a distinctive "knowledge lost" chime/crash sound

6. STRATEGIC IMPLICATIONS

Base defense:
- Protecting Town Centers is now critical beyond just avoiding defeat
- Walls and garrisons around TCs become more valuable
- Players must balance aggressive expansion with TC defense

Forward bases:
- Building a forward TC for map control is a calculated risk — if it falls, you lose tech
- This creates a meaningful downside to aggressive expansion that doesn't exist in most RTS games
- "Should I build a forward TC?" becomes a strategic question with real consequences

Raiding strategy:
- Targeting enemy TCs becomes a dual-purpose tactic: deny territory AND regress their tech
- A surgical TC snipe can be more valuable than killing 20 units
- Siege units gain strategic importance beyond just building damage

Singularity defense:
- In the late game, a player pursuing Singularity victory has the most to lose from TC destruction
- Losing the Transformer Architecture tech forces a costly re-research
- This makes the Singularity path high-risk/high-reward: you need an advanced tech tree AND you need to defend it
- Other players are incentivized to target the tech leader's TCs

Comeback mechanic:
- A player who falls behind militarily but leads in tech can be pulled back by TC raids
- Creates natural rubber-banding without artificial handicaps
- A successful defense of your TCs under pressure is genuinely rewarding

7. EDGE CASES AND LIMITS
- Maximum 1 tech lost per TC destruction (not cascading)
- If multiple TCs are destroyed in rapid succession (<5 seconds apart), each triggers a separate tech loss
- If the lost tech was a prerequisite for another researched tech: the dependent tech is NOT lost (only the most recent one). The prerequisite chain is not recursively unwound.
- During an age advancement in progress at the destroyed TC: the age advancement is cancelled and resources refunded (but no tech is lost from the cancellation — the trigger is TC destruction, not research cancellation)
- AI players also lose tech when their TCs are destroyed (no AI immunity)

8. BALANCE TUNING
The severity can be tuned via data:
- data/settings/knowledge_burning.json:
  {
    "enabled": true,
    "trigger": "town_center_destroyed",
    "tech_loss_count": 1,
    "protect_age_advancements": true,
    "protect_first_n_techs": 0,
    "re_research_cost_multiplier": 1.0,
    "cooldown_seconds": 0
  }
- protect_first_n_techs: could be set to 2-3 to protect the earliest techs (gentler for new players)
- re_research_cost_multiplier: could be 0.5 for half-price re-research (less punishing)
- cooldown_seconds: could prevent multiple tech losses within N seconds (prevents total wipeout from a coordinated TC rush)

CONSEQUENCES
- Town Centers become the highest-value targets in the game
- Every TC construction is a strategic decision (expansion vs risk)
- The tech tree has genuine stakes — progress is not permanent
- Late-game Singularity pushes require serious military defense
- Creates natural comeback mechanics through targeted raids
- Adds emotional weight to city destruction (players will FEEL the loss)
- AI must factor TC defense into its strategy
- The mechanic is easy to explain but creates deep strategic ripples
title: ADR-011: Knowledge Burning — Tech Regression on City Destruction

description: Extends the debug HTTP server (from epic a15) with entity inspection, conditional waiting, scenario scripting, entity spawning, and annotated screenshots. Enables Claude Code to fully observe and control game state for debugging and testing.
title: [epic] Debug server v2: entity inspection and game automation

description:
Add GET /entities endpoint to the debug server that returns structured data about all game entities.

## Query parameters
- category — filter by entity_category (resource_node, unit, building)
- type — filter by resource_type or unit_type (tree, fish, villager, etc.)
- owner — filter by owner_id (0=player, 1=AI, -1=gaia)

## Response fields per entity
- name, entity_category, position (grid and screen), owner_id
- For units: unit_type, current action/state (idle, gathering, moving, combat), gather_state, gather_type, carried_amount, carry_capacity, selected, hp
- For resources: resource_type, resource_name, current_yield, total_yield, grid_position
- For buildings: building_type, hp, is_drop_off, drop_off_types

## CLI wrapper
- \`./tools/ror entities [--category X] [--type Y] [--owner N]\`
- Pretty-print table or JSON output

This is the highest-impact addition — enables Claude Code to inspect game state beyond raw pixel screenshots.
title: Entity query endpoint (GET /entities)

description:
Add \`./tools/ror wait-until <condition> [--timeout N]\` that polls GET /status until a condition is met.

## Conditions
- Resource comparisons: \`wood>250\`, \`food>=100\`
- Time: \`time>120\`
- Unit state: \`idle>3\` (more than 3 idle units)

## Behavior
- Polls every 1s (configurable with --interval)
- Default timeout 60s
- Exits 0 on success, 1 on timeout
- Prints final status on exit

Enables scripted waits between game-cmd actions without manual polling.
title: Conditional wait CLI (ror wait-until)

description:
Add \`./tools/ror scenario <script>\` that executes a semicolon-separated sequence of debug commands with built-in waits.

## Syntax
- Commands: select-all, gather X Y, right-click X Y, camera-to X Y, speed N, screenshot NAME, wait CONDITION
- Example: \`select-all; gather 5 4; wait wood>250; screenshot drop-off-complete\`

## Features
- Sequential execution with error handling (stop on first failure)
- Optional --file flag to read from a .scenario file
- Print step-by-step progress

Enables reproducible multi-step test sequences for visual bug verification.
title: Scenario runner (ror scenario)

description:
Add spawn and teleport actions to POST /command endpoint.

## Actions
- spawn: create entity at grid position
  - \`{"action": "spawn", "type": "villager", "grid_x": 5, "grid_y": 5, "owner": 0}\`
  - \`{"action": "spawn", "type": "tree", "grid_x": 8, "grid_y": 8}\`
  - Support: villager, tree, stone, gold, fish, and any unit/resource type in data/
- teleport: move selected units to grid position instantly (no pathfinding)
  - \`{"action": "teleport", "grid_x": 5, "grid_y": 5}\`

## CLI
- \`./tools/ror game-cmd spawn type=villager grid_x=5 grid_y=5 owner=0\`
- \`./tools/ror game-cmd teleport grid_x=5 grid_y=5\`

Enables setting up specific test scenarios without relying on random map generation.
title: Spawn and teleport commands

description:
Add GET /screenshot?annotate=true that overlays debug information on the viewport capture.

## Annotations
- Entity name labels above each unit/resource/building
- Unit state text (idle, gathering wood, moving, carrying 8 wood)
- Health bars
- Grid coordinate overlay on tiles
- Selection highlight (bright outline on selected units)

## Implementation options
- Option A: Draw annotations in GDScript before capture (temporary CanvasLayer), remove after
- Option B: Post-process the PNG in Python (add text overlays via PIL)

Option A is more accurate (uses game coordinates). Option B is simpler and doesn't modify game state.

Solves the problem of multiple units stacked on the same tile being indistinguishable in raw screenshots.
title: Annotated screenshot mode

description:
Implement the river transport system: resources deposited at upstream River Docks are automatically shipped downstream via barges.

Core transport logic:
1. When a villager deposits resources at a River Dock, check for a valid downstream route:
   - Trace river tiles following flow_direction from the dock's adjacent river tile
   - Look for the nearest downstream building that is a valid drop-off (another River Dock owned by same player, or a Town Center within 2 tiles of the river)
   - If found, initiate a barge shipment
   - If no downstream depot exists, resources just stay in the player stockpile as normal (the River Dock still functions as a regular drop-off)

2. Barge entity:
   - Visual: small sprite moving along river tiles following flow_direction
   - Speed: 3x base villager movement speed (configurable via river_dock.json transport_speed_multiplier)
   - Capacity: 30 resources per trip (configurable via max_barge_capacity)
   - Dispatch interval: every 5 seconds, if resources are waiting at the dock (configurable via barge_spawn_interval)
   - Batching: barge carries up to capacity of mixed resource types deposited since last dispatch

3. Resource flow accounting:
   - When barge departs: resources are already in player stockpile (deposited by villager at dock)
   - The barge is purely visual + vulnerability — resources are not "held" by the barge for accounting purposes
   - HOWEVER: if the barge is destroyed, the carried resource amount is DEDUCTED from the player stockpile
   - This creates risk: fast transport but vulnerable to raiding
   - Display "in transit" amount in resource UI as a secondary indicator

4. Barge destruction:
   - Barges have 15 HP and 0 defense (very fragile)
   - Any unit can attack a barge (melee units must be adjacent to river, ranged can shoot from bank)
   - When destroyed: carried resources are lost (deducted from player stockpile), small splash visual
   - Destroyed barges do not respawn automatically — the dock simply dispatches a new one on its next cycle

5. Pathfinding for barges:
   - Barges follow river tiles via flow_direction — no pathfinding needed, just follow the chain
   - At river merges (two rivers join), barge continues downstream on the merged river
   - If the downstream river is blocked (enemy building on bank? destroyed bridge?), barge queues at the blockage

6. Multiple docks on same river:
   - Each dock independently dispatches barges to the nearest downstream depot
   - A chain of docks acts like a relay: Dock A ships to Dock B, Dock B ships to Town Center
   - Each hop is a separate barge

Data file: data/buildings/river_dock.json
{
  "transport_speed_multiplier": 3.0,
  "max_barge_capacity": 30,
  "barge_spawn_interval": 5.0,
  "barge_hp": 15
}

Acceptance criteria:

- Multiple docks on the same river chain correctly
- Transport speed is noticeably faster than villager walking
- System handles edge cases: dock destroyed mid-transit, no downstream depot, river with no docks

description:
Implement the barge as a visible, attackable entity that moves along rivers carrying resources.

Barge entity:
- Node type: extends the base unit entity (CharacterBody2D or Area2D)
- NOT player-controllable — moves automatically following river flow_direction
- Sprite: small boat/raft placeholder (Phase 1: brown rectangle on water)
- Size: roughly half a tile width
- Movement: follows river tiles sequentially, moving from tile center to tile center along flow_direction
- Speed: transport_speed_multiplier * base_villager_speed (default 3x)
- Shows small resource icons above the barge indicating what it carries

Combat properties:
- HP: 15 (very fragile — 1-2 hits from most units)
- Defense: 0
- Targetable by any unit (melee must be adjacent to river tile, ranged can attack from bank)
- Does NOT fight back
- When destroyed: splash particle effect, resource icons scatter and fade
- Resource loss: on destruction, the carried resource amounts are deducted from the owning player's stockpile
- The player receives a notification: "Barge destroyed! Lost: 20 Gold, 10 Stone"

Visual feedback:
- Barge has a small wake/ripple trail behind it
- Carries a tiny player-color flag (team color shader applies)
- When taking damage: flash red
- Resource load shown as small stacked icons on the barge sprite
- Empty barges are not spawned — only dispatched when resources are queued

Lifecycle:
1. Spawned at River Dock when dispatch timer fires and resources are queued
2. Resources loaded (up to barge_capacity)
3. Barge moves along river tiles following flow_direction chain
4. On reaching destination drop-off: barge fades out, no resource change needed (resources already in stockpile)
5. On destruction: resources deducted, barge removed, dock can dispatch a new one next cycle

Barge pooling: for performance, reuse barge nodes rather than creating/destroying (max ~20 barges on screen at once expected).

Acceptance criteria:
- Barges visually move along river following flow direction
- Barges display carried resource types
- Barges can be selected (for info) but not commanded
- Enemy units can attack and destroy barges
- Resource deduction occurs on barge destruction with player notification
- Player color visible on barges
- Smooth movement along curved river paths
- No performance issues with up to 20 simultaneous barges
title: Barge entity: visual, movement, and combat vulnerability

description:
Implement the Feed command that allows villagers to domesticate wolves.

Feed command:
- Available when: a villager is selected and the cursor is over a wolf
- Activation: Shift+right-click on wolf, or "Feed" button in command panel (only visible when hovering/targeting a wolf)
- Cursor changes to a food/hand icon when hovering a wolf with a villager selected
- Cost: 25 Food per feeding, deducted from stockpile when villager begins walking to wolf

Feeding process:
1. Villager checks if player has 25 Food — if not, error sound + "Not enough food" message
2. 25 Food deducted from player stockpile
3. Villager walks toward wolf, stopping at 2-tile distance (outside aggro radius of 3)
4. Villager plays feeding animation (extend arm) for 5 seconds
5. Wolf enters BEING_FED state, plays eating animation
6. After 5 seconds: wolf.domestication_progress += 0.33
7. wolf.fed_by = player_id (track who is domesticating this wolf)
8. If domestication_progress >= 1.0: wolf transforms into Dog for this player
9. If domestication_progress < 1.0: wolf returns to PATROL, villager returns to previous task

Domestication progress:
- 3 feedings required (each adds 33.3%, stored as float 0.0 to 1.0)
- Progress decays at 10% per minute if not fed (decay_interval: 60 seconds)
- Progress displayed as a small bar above the wolf when hovered
- If a different player feeds the wolf: progress resets and fed_by changes to the new player (contested domestication)

Wolf-to-Dog transformation:
- At progress >= 1.0, remove the wolf entity from Gaia
- Spawn a Dog entity at the same position, owned by the feeding player
- Small transformation visual: sparkle/glow effect, wolf sprite morphs to dog sprite
- Notification: "Wolf domesticated! You gained a Dog companion."
- Packmates of the domesticated wolf do NOT automatically become friendly — they remain wild

Tooltip on wolf hover (for any player):
- "Wolf — Wild fauna"
- "Can be domesticated by feeding (Shift+Right Click with villager)"
- "Cost: 25 Food per feeding (3 feedings required)"
- Domestication bar showing current progress and owner
- "Can be hunted for 50 Food"

Edge cases:
- Villager killed while feeding: feeding interrupted, food already spent (lost), wolf returns to PATROL
- Wolf killed while being fed: feeding interrupted, food already spent, wolf drops 50 Food corpse
- Two villagers feeding same wolf simultaneously: only one feed counts per 5-second window
- Wolf attacked by another player while being fed: feeding interrupted, wolf enters FLEE

Acceptance criteria:
- Feed command visible and usable with villager selected + wolf targeted
- 25 Food deducted per feeding
- Domestication progress bar visible on wolf
- 3 feedings transforms wolf to player-owned dog
- Progress decays over time if not maintained
- Contested domestication resets progress to new player
- Tooltip explains the mechanic to new players
- Transformation has visual/audio feedback
title: Wolf feeding command and domestication process

Drop-off functionality:

description:
## Problem

Claude Code cannot observe the running game. When debugging visual issues (z-ordering, sprite rendering, unit positioning), the user must manually take screenshots and save them for Claude to review. This breaks the feedback loop and slows iteration.

The existing `tools/screenshot_helper.gd` only captures static screenshots at startup — it cannot interact with a running game instance.

## Goal

A tool that lets Claude Code capture a screenshot of the running Godot game on demand, so it can observe the visual state during an active play session. This enables a tighter debug loop: launch game → perform action → screenshot → analyze → iterate.

## Design

### Architecture: GDScript HTTP server inside the game

Add a lightweight HTTP debug server that runs inside the game process (dev builds only). Claude Code sends HTTP requests to capture screenshots and (optionally) perform basic game actions.

### Components

**1. `scripts/debug/debug_server.gd` — In-game HTTP server (autoload, dev-only)**
- Listens on `localhost:9222` (configurable)
- Only active when launched with `--debug-server` user arg or `OS.is_debug_build()`
- Endpoints:
  - `GET /screenshot` — captures viewport, returns PNG bytes (or saves to path and returns JSON with path)
  - `GET /status` — returns JSON: game tick, player resources, unit count, selected units, camera position
  - `POST /command` — execute simple game actions: select-all, right-click at grid position, pan camera, set game speed
- Minimal footprint — no external dependencies, uses Godot's built-in `TCPServer`/`HTTPServer` or `StreamPeerTCP`

**2. `tools/ror screenshot` — CLI wrapper**
- Sends `GET /screenshot` to the debug server
- Saves PNG to `tests/screenshots/<name>.png`
- Returns the file path so Claude Code can `Read` it
- Usage: `./tools/ror screenshot [--output path] [--name label]`

**3. `tools/ror game-status` — Game state query**
- Sends `GET /status` to the debug server
- Returns JSON summary of current game state

**4. `tools/ror game-cmd` — Send commands to running game**
- `./tools/ror game-cmd select-all`
- `./tools/ror game-cmd right-click 5,10` (grid coords)
- `./tools/ror game-cmd camera-to 5,10`
- `./tools/ror game-cmd speed 2`

**5. Update `/launch` skill** to enable the debug server automatically

### Security

- Bind to `127.0.0.1` only (localhost)
- Only active in debug builds or when explicitly enabled via cmdline arg
- No file system write access beyond screenshot output directory

### Integration with Claude Code

After this tool ships, the workflow becomes:
1. `/launch` — starts game with debug server enabled
2. `./tools/ror screenshot --name before_fix` — Claude reads the PNG
3. Make code changes
4. `./tools/ror game-cmd select-all && ./tools/ror game-cmd right-click 5,10`
5. Wait a few seconds
6. `./tools/ror screenshot --name after_fix` — Claude verifies the fix visually

### Stories (suggested breakdown)

1. **Debug HTTP server** — TCP listener + `/screenshot` endpoint
2. **`/status` endpoint** — game state query
3. **`/command` endpoint** — basic remote control (select, right-click, camera)
4. **CLI wrappers** — `ror screenshot`, `ror game-status`, `ror game-cmd`
5. **`/launch` integration** — auto-enable debug server, update skill
title: Tool: in-game screenshot capture for Claude Code

description:
Create `scripts/debug/debug_server.gd` — a lightweight TCP server autoload that listens on localhost:9222 and serves a `/screenshot` endpoint.

## Acceptance criteria

- GDScript autoload registered in project.godot
- Only activates when `--debug-server` is in `OS.get_cmdline_user_args()` (no-op otherwise)
- Binds to `127.0.0.1:9222` only
- `GET /screenshot` captures the viewport and returns raw PNG bytes with `Content-Type: image/png`
- `GET /ping` returns 200 OK (health check)
- Handles concurrent connections gracefully (reject if busy)
- Minimal `_process` polling — check for connections each frame only when server is active
- Tests: unit tests for request parsing and response formatting
title: Debug HTTP server with /screenshot endpoint

description:
Generate 4 grass tile variants at 1024x1024 using image generation.

Prompt:

Isometric diamond tile for a 2D RTS game. The tile is a perfect diamond (rhombus) shape with a 2:1 width-to-height ratio, viewed from a top-down isometric camera angle (approximately 30 degrees from horizontal). Pixel art style with clean edges. Transparent background outside the diamond. No objects, units, or structures — terrain surface only. The tile should seamlessly connect with identical copies of itself on all four edges.

Lush green grassland with subtle variation in shade. Short grass texture with occasional tiny wildflowers. Warm sunlight from the upper-left. Colors: spring greens (#5B8C3E, #7DB356, #4A7A2E).

Output: 4 variants at 1024x1024, downscale to 128x64 for final asset. Save to assets/tiles/terrain/grass_*.png
title: Generate terrain tile art: grass
