description:
Create a Python script (tools/generate_tiles.py) that procedurally generates the entire Phase 1 placeholder tileset. Zero manual drawing.

The script generates 128x64px isometric diamond tiles using PIL/Pillow:
- Grass: green fill (#4A7C4A) with 1px darker border
- Forest floor: dark green (#3D5A3D) with small triangle shapes suggesting trees
- Desert: tan fill (#D4B87A) with subtle dot pattern
- Shallow water: light blue (#5B9BD5) with wavy line pattern
- Deep water: dark blue (#2E5B8A) with darker wavy lines
- Mountain: gray (#8C8C8C) with triangle peaks
- Elevation ramp: gradient from low to high color

For terrain autotiling, generate transition variants:
- Use Godot 4 TileMap terrain peering bit system
- For each terrain pair, generate edge tiles by splitting the diamond into regions and filling each with the appropriate terrain color
- Generate: center, 4 cardinal edges, 4 corners, 4 inner corners = 13 variants per terrain type
- Total output: ~65-80 tile PNGs

Also generate resource node sprites (32x32 on tile):
- Gold: yellow circle with G label
- Stone: gray hexagon with S label
- Wood: brown rectangle (tree trunk)
- Berry bush: red dots cluster
- Fish: blue fish shape on water
- Deer: brown triangle

Script outputs:
- Individual PNGs to assets/tiles/terrain/
- A Godot .tres TileSet resource file configured with terrain sets and peering bits
- A manifest JSON listing all generated tiles with metadata

Script is idempotent — running it again overwrites with identical output.
Add to CI: regenerate and diff to ensure committed tiles match generator output.

Acceptance criteria:
- python3 tools/generate_tiles.py runs without dependencies beyond Pillow
- Generates 60+ tile PNGs that load correctly in Godot TileMap
- Terrain autotiling produces smooth transitions in editor
- All 5 terrain types + resources visually distinct at game zoom
- CI validates generated output matches committed assets
title: Procedural tileset generator — Phase 1 terrain tiles

description:
Create a Python script (tools/spritesheet_packer.py) that takes rendered individual frame PNGs and produces packed spritesheets plus Godot SpriteFrames resources.

Input: directory of PNGs following naming convention {animation}_{direction}_{frame}.png, plus manifest.json from the render script.

Output per unit/building:
1. Packed spritesheet PNG — all frames arranged in a grid (rows = animations, columns = frames). Tightly packed with no wasted space. Power-of-2 texture dimensions preferred but not required.
2. Spritesheet metadata JSON — frame positions, sizes, animation names, frame durations.
3. Godot SpriteFrames .tres resource file — directly importable by Godot, with:
   - Named animations: idle_n, idle_ne, idle_e, idle_se, idle_s, idle_sw, idle_w, idle_nw, walk_n, walk_ne, ..., attack_n, ..., death
   - Frame references pointing to atlas regions in the spritesheet
   - Animation FPS (configurable, default 10fps for idle, 12fps for walk, 15fps for attack)
   - Loop flag per animation (idle/walk loop, attack/death don't loop)

Packing algorithm:
- Simple grid packing (all frames same bounding box, padded to largest frame)
- Trim transparent pixels per frame before packing (store offset for reconstruction)
- Output both trimmed spritesheet and a padding-info JSON for Godot to reconstruct positions

CLI: python3 tools/spritesheet_packer.py --input renders/infantry/ --output assets/sprites/units/infantry/ --name infantry --fps-idle 10 --fps-walk 12 --fps-attack 15

Batch mode: python3 tools/spritesheet_packer.py --batch renders/ --output assets/sprites/ (processes all subdirectories)

Acceptance criteria:
- Packs frames into spritesheet with zero visual artifacts
- Generated .tres file loads in Godot editor with correct animations
- Animation preview in editor plays correctly at specified FPS
- Batch mode processes all units/buildings in one command
- Transparent pixel trimming reduces spritesheet size by at least 30% vs naive packing
- Script completes in under 10 seconds for a full unit (all animations)
title: Build automated spritesheet packer with Godot resource generation

description:
Create a Godot shader that replaces a designated mask color with the player's team color at runtime. This eliminates the need to render separate sprite variants per player.

Approach:
- All unit and building sprites are rendered with magenta (#FF00FF) as the team-color region
- A fragment shader detects pixels matching the mask color (with tolerance for anti-aliasing)
- Replaces mask pixels with the player's assigned color, preserving luminance/shading
- Shader uniform: team_color (Color) — set per-unit based on owning player
- Shader uniform: mask_tolerance (float, default 0.1) — how close to magenta counts as mask

Shader must handle:
- Anti-aliased edges: mask region borders will have blended pixels. Use smooth interpolation between original and team color based on proximity to mask color.
- Shading preservation: a dark magenta pixel should become a dark version of the team color, not flat team color. Preserve the luminance channel.
- Multiple mask regions: some units may have primary (body) and secondary (banner/shield) team color regions. Support 2 mask colors: magenta (#FF00FF) for primary, cyan (#00FFFF) for secondary.

Implementation:
- Create shader file: assets/shaders/team_color.gdshader
- Create ShaderMaterial resource: assets/shaders/team_color_material.tres
- Helper script: scripts/utils/team_color.gd with:
  - apply_team_color(sprite: Sprite2D, player_color: Color)
  - Static player color definitions matching ADR-008

Test the shader with:
- Phase 1 geometric sprites (magenta regions)
- Verify all 4 player colors look correct
- Verify shading gradients are preserved
- Verify performance: shader should add negligible cost per sprite

Acceptance criteria:
- Shader correctly recolors magenta mask to any team color
- Shading/luminance preserved (dark mask pixels become dark team color)
- Anti-aliased edges look clean (no magenta fringing)
- Works with both Sprite2D and AnimatedSprite2D
- Performance: no measurable frame time increase with 100 recolored sprites
- Unit test verifying team_color.gd helper applies shader correctly
title: Implement team color shader for runtime player color recoloring

description:
Create a validation script (tools/validate_sprites.py) that runs in CI to enforce sprite consistency rules from ADR-008.

Validations:
1. Dimension check: verify all sprites match the scale contract
   - Villager sprites: bounding box <= 64x64px (with ~48px content height)
   - Infantry: bounding box <= 72x72px
   - Cavalry: bounding box <= 80x80px
   - Building 1x1: bounding box <= 128x128px
   - Building 2x2: bounding box <= 256x192px
   - Building 3x3: bounding box <= 384x256px

2. Frame count check: verify each unit has the expected number of frames
   - Read manifest.json for each unit
   - Verify all 8 directions present for directional animations
   - Verify frame counts match animation definitions
   - Flag missing or extra frames

3. Naming convention check: verify all files follow asset naming rules
   - snake_case, lowercase
   - Match pattern: {name}_{animation}_{direction}_{frame}.png
   - No spaces, no uppercase, no special characters

4. Team color mask check: verify magenta (#FF00FF) pixels exist in team-color regions
   - Sample center pixels of units/buildings
   - Warn if no magenta mask found (unit won't recolor)

5. Transparency check: verify all sprites have transparent backgrounds (alpha channel)

6. Spritesheet integrity: verify packed spritesheets match their metadata JSON
   - Frame positions don't overlap
   - All declared frames exist in the sheet
   - Sheet dimensions match metadata

7. Regeneration check (Phase 1 only): regenerate procedural sprites and diff against committed
   - If committed sprites don't match generator output, fail CI
   - Ensures no one hand-edits generated files

Output: structured report (JSON + human-readable summary). Exit code 0 if all pass, 1 if any fail.

Add to GitHub Actions CI workflow as a step after tests but before export.

Acceptance criteria:
- Catches dimension violations
- Catches missing frames or directions
- Catches naming convention violations
- Catches missing team color mask
- Clean output format with actionable error messages
- Runs in under 5 seconds for the full asset set
- Integrated into CI pipeline
title: Add CI validation for sprite dimensions, frame counts, and naming

description:
Create a master orchestrator script (tools/asset_pipeline.py) that runs the full asset production pipeline in one command.

Usage:
  python3 tools/asset_pipeline.py --phase 1                    # Generate all Phase 1 procedural assets
  python3 tools/asset_pipeline.py --phase 2 --model infantry   # Render one model through Blender pipeline
  python3 tools/asset_pipeline.py --phase 2 --all              # Render all models
  python3 tools/asset_pipeline.py --validate                   # Run validation only
  python3 tools/asset_pipeline.py --pack                       # Re-pack all spritesheets from renders

Phase 1 pipeline (fully automated):
1. Run generate_tiles.py -> terrain tileset
2. Run generate_unit_sprites.py -> unit placeholders
3. Run generate_building_sprites.py -> building placeholders
4. Run spritesheet_packer.py --batch -> pack all into spritesheets
5. Run validate_sprites.py -> verify everything
6. Report: total assets generated, total file size, any warnings

Phase 2 pipeline (per model):
1. Find .blend file for the model in blender/models/
2. Run Blender headless with batch_render.py -> individual PNGs
3. Run spritesheet_packer.py -> pack into spritesheets + Godot resources
4. Run validate_sprites.py -> verify dimensions and frame counts
5. Report: frames rendered, spritesheet size, validation results

Pipeline configuration via tools/asset_config.json:
- List of all units with type, scale preset, animations, FPS values
- List of all buildings with type, footprint, states
- Validation thresholds and dimension limits
- Output directory mappings

The config file is the single source of truth for what assets exist and their properties.

Makefile/justfile targets for convenience:
  make assets-phase1    # Generate Phase 1
  make assets-validate  # Validate all
  make assets-pack      # Re-pack spritesheets
  make assets-clean     # Remove all generated assets

Acceptance criteria:
- Single command generates entire Phase 1 asset set from scratch
- Phase 2 pipeline renders a model through Blender and produces game-ready sprites
- Validation runs automatically after generation
- Config file is the single source of truth — adding a new unit means adding a JSON entry
- Pipeline completes Phase 1 in under 30 seconds
- Pipeline completes Phase 2 for one model in under 2 minutes
title: Create end-to-end asset pipeline orchestrator script

description:
Create a Python script (tools/generate_unit_sprites.py) that procedurally generates all Phase 1 placeholder unit sprites. Zero manual drawing.

The script generates sprites using PIL/Pillow for each unit type:

Unit definitions (shape, base size):
- Villager: circle, 24px diameter, carry-bag indicator when gathering
- Infantry: circle with flat bottom (shield), 26px
- Archer: circle with line extending outward (bow), 26px
- Cavalry: horizontal oval, 32x20px
- Siege: rectangle, 28x20px
- Naval: wide diamond, 36x16px

For each unit, generate:
- 8 directional variants (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW) — same shape with rotated direction arrow/indicator
- Idle animation: 2 frames (subtle size pulse) x 8 dirs = 16 frames
- Walk animation: 4 frames (bouncing motion via vertical offset) x 8 dirs = 32 frames
- Attack animation: 3 frames (flash + extend) x 8 dirs = 24 frames
- Death animation: 4 frames (shrink + fade) x 1 dir = 4 frames
- Total per unit: ~76 frames (simplified from 150 — Phase 1 uses fewer frames)

Player color: render with NEUTRAL color (magenta #FF00FF) as the team-color mask region.
The runtime shader will replace this with actual player color.
Also generate a preview set with Player 1 blue for visual testing.

Selection indicator: separate green circle outline sprite.
Health bar: separate sprite (green/yellow/red segments).

Output:
- Individual PNGs organized per unit: assets/sprites/units/placeholder/{unit_name}/
- Packed spritesheets per unit (one PNG + JSON frame data)
- Godot SpriteFrames .tres resource per unit with named animations
- Manifest JSON listing all units, frame counts, dimensions

Acceptance criteria:
- python3 tools/generate_unit_sprites.py generates all 6 unit types
- Each unit visually distinct from others at game zoom
- Direction arrow makes facing obvious
- Magenta team-color region clearly defined for shader swap
- SpriteFrames resources load and preview correctly in Godot editor
- CI validates output matches committed sprites
title: Procedural unit sprite generator — Phase 1 placeholder units

description:
Create a Python script (tools/generate_building_sprites.py) that procedurally generates all Phase 1 placeholder building sprites. Zero manual drawing.

Building definitions (shape, footprint, icon):
- Town Center (3x3): large rectangle 384x256, flag icon, player color banner
- House (1x1): small square 128x128, door shape
- Farm (2x2): green rectangle 256x192 with grid lines
- Barracks (2x2): rectangle 256x192, crossed swords icon
- Dock (2x1): rectangle 256x128 extending over water
- Market (2x2): rectangle 256x192, coin circle icon
- Library (2x2): rectangle 256x192, book icon
- Wonder (4x4): large distinctive octagon 512x320
- Mill (1x1): rectangle 128x128, wheat icon
- Mining Camp (1x1): rectangle 128x128, pickaxe icon
- Lumber Camp (1x1): rectangle 128x128, axe icon
- GPU Foundry (2x2): rectangle 256x192, chip icon (Information Age)
- Transformer Lab (2x2): rectangle 256x192, neural net icon (Singularity)
- AGI Core (3x3): glowing rectangle 384x256, brain icon (Singularity)

For each building generate:
- Complete state: solid fill with icon and player color banner (magenta mask)
- Construction stages: 4 frames (25/50/75/100% fill from bottom up, wireframe outline)
- Damaged overlay: red tint at <50% HP (separate overlay sprite)
- Ruins: gray desaturated outline (post-destruction)
- Ghost preview: semi-transparent green and red variants for placement

Output:
- PNGs to assets/sprites/buildings/placeholder/{building_name}/
- Packed spritesheets + Godot SpriteFrames resources
- Manifest JSON with building metadata (footprint, icon, states)

Acceptance criteria:
- All 14 building types generated and visually distinct
- Footprints match tile grid exactly (verify pixel dimensions)
- Construction progression is visually clear
- Player color mask (magenta) present on all buildings
- CI validates output
title: Procedural building sprite generator — Phase 1 placeholder buildings

description:
Create the asset directory tree and document naming conventions before any assets are committed.

Directory structure:
- assets/sprites/units/placeholder/ — Phase 1 geometric units
- assets/sprites/units/{civ}/ — Per-civ unit art (future phases)
- assets/sprites/buildings/placeholder/ — Phase 1 geometric buildings
- assets/sprites/buildings/{civ}/ — Per-civ buildings (future phases)
- assets/tiles/terrain/ — Tileset textures
- assets/tiles/elevation/ — Elevation and cliff tiles
- assets/tiles/transitions/ — Terrain transition tiles
- assets/ui/icons/ — Resource icons, button icons
- assets/ui/panels/ — HUD panel backgrounds
- assets/ui/fonts/ — Game fonts
- assets/effects/ — Particle textures, projectiles
- assets/portraits/ — Unit/building portrait art for info panel

Naming convention (document in assets/README.md):
- All snake_case, lowercase
- Units: {unit_name}_{animation}_{direction}_{frame}.png (e.g., villager_walk_se_03.png)
- Spritesheets: {unit_name}_{animation}_sheet.png (packed)
- Buildings: {building_name}_{state}.png (e.g., town_center_complete.png, barracks_construction_02.png)
- Tiles: {terrain}_{variant}_{index}.png (e.g., grass_center_01.png, grass_edge_n_01.png)
- UI: {element}_{state}.png (e.g., button_normal.png, button_pressed.png)

Also create:
- assets/.gdignore for any source files (Aseprite .ase) that shouldn't be imported
- Godot import presets: pixel art (no filtering, no mipmaps) as default for all sprites
- Document sprite scale contract from ADR-008 in assets/README.md

Acceptance criteria:
- All directories created
- README.md documents conventions with examples
- Godot import defaults configured for pixel art
- One sample asset in each directory category (can be 1x1 pixel placeholder)
title: Establish asset directory structure and naming conventions

description:

- Player 4: Orange (#F39C12)

description:
Create minimal UI assets so HUD development can proceed without final art.

Resource icons (32x32px each, simple geometric):

- Wood: brown log rectangle
- Stone: gray rock triangle
- Gold: yellow coin circle
- Knowledge: blue book square
- Population: white person silhouette

HUD panel backgrounds:
- Bottom panel: dark semi-transparent rectangle (#1A1A1A at 80% opacity)
- Minimap frame: dark border rectangle
- Resource bar: dark strip with slight gradient
- Tooltip background: dark with 1px border

Button states (64x32px):

- Hover: slightly lighter
- Pressed: slightly darker, 1px inset
- Disabled: desaturated

Cursor sprites:

- Move: boot/arrow pointing down
- Attack: sword/crosshair
- Gather: hand/pick
- Build: hammer

description:
Build a quick throwaway prototype (separate scene, not production code) to validate the isometric feel before building real systems. This is a time-boxed spike — max 1 day of effort.

Prototype must answer these questions:
1. Does 128x64 tile size feel right at target zoom? Can you see enough of the map?
2. Does the camera pan/zoom feel responsive? What are good min/max zoom limits?
3. Is a villager at ~48px tall visible and selectable without squinting?
4. Does unit movement speed feel right? How many tiles per second is good?
5. Can you visually distinguish 3-4 different terrain types at game zoom?
6. Does the isometric click-to-world coordinate conversion feel accurate?

Build:
- Hand-place a 20x20 isometric tilemap with 3-4 terrain types using placeholder tiles
- Add a Camera2D with pan (WASD) and zoom (scroll wheel)
- Place 5-10 unit placeholders (colored circles)
- Click to select, right-click to move (basic, no pathfinding — just lerp to position)
- Isometric screen-to-world coordinate conversion for click targeting

Document findings in a short writeup:
- What tile size / zoom range felt good
- What unit speed felt good (tiles per second)
- Any surprises about iso coordinate math
- Screenshots at min zoom, default zoom, max zoom

This prototype is explicitly throwaway — it does NOT need tests, lint, or coverage. It informs ADR decisions before real implementation begins.

Acceptance criteria:
- Can pan and zoom around a 20x20 iso map
- Can click to select and right-click to move a unit
- Findings documented with specific numbers (zoom range, speed, etc.)
- Prototype clearly marked as throwaway (separate directory or branch)
title: Build throwaway isometric feel prototype

description:
Create a Blender template scene (blender/render_rig.blend) that automates rendering 3D models into isometric 2D sprites from 8 directions.

Render rig specification:
- 8 orthographic cameras arranged at 45-degree intervals around the origin
- Camera naming: cam_n, cam_ne, cam_e, cam_se, cam_s, cam_sw, cam_w, cam_nw
- All cameras at isometric angle: 30 degrees from horizontal (atan(1/sqrt(2)) = 35.264 degrees technically, but 30 is the standard iso approximation — test both, pick what matches 128x64 tiles)
- Orthographic projection with scale calibrated so a standing humanoid renders at ~48px tall (villager scale) in the output
- Consistent 3-point lighting: key light (warm, upper-left), fill light (cool, lower-right), rim light (behind, subtle)
- Transparent background (RGBA PNG output)
- Render resolution: 256x256px per frame (2x target size for downscale quality, final sprites are 128x128 max bounding box)
- Output format: PNG with alpha, 8-bit color

Camera calibration process:
- Place a reference cube at origin matching 1-tile footprint
- Render from all 8 cameras
- Verify the cube renders as a proper 128x64 isometric diamond when downscaled
- Save camera transforms as constants in the batch render script

Include in the .blend file:
- Empty at origin as the model placement target
- Reference cube (hidden by default) for scale calibration
- Collection structure: Cameras, Lights, Model (empty, user places their model here)
- World settings: transparent background

Document in blender/README.md:
- How to import a model and place it in the rig
- How to adjust scale for different unit sizes (villager vs cavalry vs building)
- Camera angle rationale and how it maps to tile geometry

Acceptance criteria:
- Render rig produces correct isometric angle matching 128x64 tile geometry
- All 8 directions render with consistent lighting and framing
- Transparent background on all output
- Reference cube renders as proper iso diamond
- A test model (Blender default monkey/suzanne) renders correctly from all 8 cameras
title: Build Blender isometric render rig with 8-camera setup

description:
Create a Blender Python script (blender/batch_render.py) that automates rendering all animations of a model from all 8 cameras.

Usage: blender --background render_rig.blend --python batch_render.py -- --model infantry --animations idle,walk,attack,death --output-dir ./renders/infantry/

The script:
1. Loads the render rig scene
2. Imports or links the specified model into the Model collection
3. For each animation in the model's action list (or specified via CLI):
   a. Set the animation as active
   b. Determine frame range from action
   c. For each camera (8 directions):
      - Set active camera
      - For each frame in the animation:
        - Set frame
        - Render to PNG: {output_dir}/{animation}_{direction}_{frame:02d}.png
4. Generate a manifest.json listing all rendered files with metadata:
   - animation name, direction, frame index, file path, dimensions

Scale presets (passed via CLI --scale):
- villager: ortho scale calibrated for ~48px tall output
- infantry: ~52px tall
- cavalry: ~64px tall
- building_1x1: ~128px wide
- building_2x2: ~256px wide
- building_3x3: ~384px wide

CLI arguments:
- --model: name for output organization
- --blend-file: path to .blend file with the model (if not in render rig)
- --animations: comma-separated animation names (or 'all')
- --scale: preset name or custom ortho scale value
- --output-dir: where to write PNGs
- --directions: override which cameras to use (default: all 8)
- --resolution: override render resolution (default: 256x256)
- --downscale: final sprite size after downscale (default: 128x128 bounding box)

The script handles downscaling from render resolution to final sprite size using high-quality Lanczos resampling (via Pillow post-process or Blender compositor).

Acceptance criteria:
- Script runs headless: blender --background --python batch_render.py
- Renders all animations from all 8 cameras without manual intervention
- Output file naming matches asset convention exactly
- Manifest JSON is complete and accurate
- Renders complete in under 60 seconds for a simple model with 3 animations
- Script is idempotent — same input produces identical output
title: Create Blender batch render script for automated sprite export

description:
## Context

The game needs developer tooling for: (a) manually reproducing and debugging gameplay issues, (b) fast-forwarding to late-game scenarios without playing through early game, (c) fine-grained integration tests that set up complex multi-system scenarios. Currently, the only debug tool is DebugServer (HTTP API with /economy, /combat-log, /pathfinding, /perf endpoints). This works for data inspection but cannot mutate game state.

## Decision

### Architecture: In-Game Debug Console + Scriptable Test API

**Two complementary interfaces, one shared backend:**

1. **DebugConsole (in-game overlay)** — for manual interactive debugging
   - Toggle with backtick/tilde key (\`)
   - Translucent overlay at top of screen, does not pause game
   - Text input with command history (up/down arrows)
   - Auto-complete for commands, unit types, building types, tech names
   - Output log area shows command results and errors

2. **DebugAPI (GDScript API)** — for automated integration tests
   - Static methods callable from test scripts: DebugAPI.spawn_unit(), DebugAPI.set_resources(), etc.
   - Same command implementations as console, different entry point
   - No UI dependency — works in headless test runner

3. **DebugCommandRegistry (shared backend)** — command parsing and execution
   - Commands registered as Dictionary entries: {name, args_spec, handler_callable, help_text}
   - Each command validates args, executes against live game state, returns result string
   - All mutations go through existing system APIs (GameManager, ResourceManager, etc.) — no direct state hacking

### Command Categories

| Category | Commands | Notes |
|----------|----------|-------|
| **Spawn** | spawn \<unit_type\> [player] [count] [x,y], build \<building_type\> [player] [x,y] | Uses existing unit/building creation paths. Bypasses cost checks. |
| **Control** | move \<id\> \<x,y\>, attack \<id\> \<target\>, gather \<id\> \<resource\>, set-hp \<id\> \<hp\>, kill \<id\>, teleport \<id\> \<x,y\> | Issues commands through CommandHandler (same path as player input). |
| **Economy** | give \<resource\> \<amount\> [player], give-all \<amount\>, set-pop-cap \<amount\> | Mutates via ResourceManager/PopulationManager. |
| **Tech** | research \<tech_id\>, research-all, advance-age [target_age], set-age \<age\> | Uses TechManager.complete_research() to properly trigger effects. |
| **Vision** | reveal-map, fog on/off, show-ai | Toggles VisibilityManager state. |
| **Time** | speed \<multiplier\>, pause, step [ticks], skip \<seconds\> | Modifies Engine.time_scale or GameManager.game_speed. |
| **Overlays** | show pathfinding/los/fog/spatial-index/attack-ranges, hide \<overlay\> | Renders debug visualization layers on game world. |
| **Query** | list units [player], list buildings [player], info \<id\>, stats | Read-only inspection. |

### File Organization

- autoloads/debug_console.gd — DebugConsole autoload (UI + command input)
- scripts/debug/debug_api.gd — DebugAPI static class (test-facing)
- scripts/debug/debug_command_registry.gd — Shared command registry + execution
- scripts/debug/debug_overlays.gd — Visual debug overlay rendering
- tests/helpers/scenario_builder.gd — Test helper wrapping DebugAPI for scenario setup

### Release Build Behavior

- **Debug console is STRIPPED from release exports.** Use Godot's feature tags: all debug files wrapped in \`if OS.is_debug_build()\` guards.
- DebugConsole autoload registered conditionally in project.godot (or via a debug-only scene)
- DebugAPI remains available in debug builds only — tests never run against release builds
- No debug command should have side effects that could affect game balance if somehow invoked in release

### Integration with Existing DebugServer

- DebugServer (HTTP API) continues to serve read-only endpoints
- New write endpoints added to DebugServer that delegate to DebugCommandRegistry: POST /spawn, POST /research, etc.
- This enables external tool integration (e.g., a web dashboard that controls the game)

### ScenarioBuilder Test Helper

ScenarioBuilder wraps DebugAPI for readable test setup:
\`\`\`gdscript
var scenario = ScenarioBuilder.new()
scenario.set_civ(1, "rome").set_civ(2, "polynesia")
scenario.set_age(1, "medieval").set_age(2, "medieval")
scenario.give_resources(1, {food=1000, gold=500})
scenario.spawn_units(1, "infantry", 10, Vector2i(20, 20))
scenario.spawn_units(2, "archer", 10, Vector2i(25, 20))
scenario.build(1, "barracks", Vector2i(18, 20))
# Now assert combat outcomes...
\`\`\`

## Consequences

- Two entry points (console + API) share one implementation — no logic duplication
- Commands go through existing system APIs — debug tools stay correct as systems evolve
- Stripped from release — zero performance/security impact on shipped game
- ScenarioBuilder enables readable, maintainable integration tests
- HTTP integration enables future external tooling (web debug dashboard, CI smoke tests)
- Command registry is extensible — new systems register their own debug commands
title: ADR-023: Debug Console & Integration Test Architecture

description:
Every system in Roots of Reason must be testable and tested before merge. The CI pipeline is a first-class game system that enforces quality gates on every PR.

Decisions: (1) GdUnit4 as the unit/integration test framework (GDScript-native, JUnit XML output, CI-friendly). (2) Minimum 90% line coverage on all gameplay scripts (autoloads/, scripts/). Coverage enforced in CI — PRs that drop below threshold are blocked. (3) Static analysis via gdtoolkit (gdlint + gdformat) — zero warnings policy. (4) Headless scene smoke tests — every scene must load without errors in headless mode. (5) Playability integration tests — automated scenarios that simulate real gameplay headless and assert game state correctness. (6) Screenshot regression testing — capture UI screenshots in CI and compare against baselines. (7) Performance budgets — frame time benchmarks for key scenarios, CI fails if benchmarks regress beyond 15%. (8) Export validation — CI builds macOS and Windows exports and verifies they launch.

Consequences: High confidence in every merge. Slower CI runs (~5-10 min) but catches regressions immediately. Test infrastructure built once, pays dividends on every subsequent epic. 90% coverage enforces testable architecture. Screenshot tests catch UI regressions that unit tests miss.
title: ADR-007: Quality Pipeline — Test-Driven Game Development

description:
Rivers are not just terrain obstacles or naval highways — they are economic infrastructure. A civilization that controls river access gains a significant gathering advantage for nearby mines and quarries.

DECISION

1. RIVER GENERATION
Rivers are generated during map creation as continuous paths of shallow water tiles flowing from high elevation (mountains/hills) to low elevation (ocean/lake). Rivers have FLOW DIRECTION encoded per tile — each river tile knows which direction water flows (toward the river mouth). Rivers branch and merge realistically using a simple hydrology simulation:
- Start at mountain/hill tiles (watershed sources)
- Flow downhill following steepest gradient
- Merge when two rivers meet
- Terminate at ocean, lake, or map edge
- River width: 1 tile for streams, 2 tiles for major rivers (wider near mouth)
- Typical map has 2-4 rivers depending on size
Rivers are a distinct terrain sub-type of water: "river" vs "ocean" vs "lake". River tiles are shallow and crossable by land units at reduced speed (50% movement penalty), unlike ocean which requires ships.

2. RIVER DOCK BUILDING
A new building: River Dock (1x1 footprint, must be placed on a tile adjacent to a river tile).
- Cost: 100 Wood (cheap, available from Bronze Age)
- Build time: 30 seconds
- HP: 400 (fragile — can be raided)
- Acts as a resource drop-off point for Food, Wood, Stone, Gold (same as Mill/Mining Camp/Lumber Camp but universal)
- Additionally acts as a RIVER TRANSPORT ORIGIN: resources deposited here are automatically shipped downstream

3. RIVER TRANSPORT MECHANIC
When a villager deposits resources at a River Dock:
(a) If there is another River Dock OR Town Center downstream along the same river, the resources are automatically transported to the NEAREST downstream drop-off.
(b) Transport is visualized as a small barge sprite moving along the river tiles following flow direction.
(c) Transport speed: 3x villager walking speed (resources arrive at downstream depot much faster than a villager could walk them).
(d) Transport is NOT instant — the barge physically travels, taking time proportional to river distance.
(e) Resources are "in transit" during transport — they are deducted from the origin dock and added to the player stockpile only when the barge arrives at the downstream depot.
(f) Barges can be attacked and sunk by enemy units positioned along the river, causing resource loss (the carried resources are destroyed).

4. PROXIMITY BONUS
Villagers gathering from a resource node within 3 tiles of a River Dock will automatically use the River Dock as their drop-off IF it is closer than their next best drop-off. This is not a special mechanic — it falls out naturally from the "nearest drop-off" gathering logic since the River Dock IS a drop-off building.
The real advantage is the DOWNSTREAM TRANSPORT: resources deposited at an upstream River Dock flow downstream to your base automatically, meaning your villagers have a very short walk (to the nearby River Dock) instead of a long walk (back to the Town Center).

5. STRATEGIC IMPLICATIONS
- River Dock placement is a key strategic decision: build them upstream near mines
- Rivers that flow TOWARD your base are extremely valuable map features
- Rivers that flow AWAY from your base have no transport benefit (you'd be shipping resources away from your economy)
- Enemy River Docks are high-value raid targets — destroying them forces villagers to walk the long way
- Barges on the river are vulnerable to archers or units positioned on the banks
- Controlling both banks of a river upstream of your enemy denies them this economic advantage
- Mesopotamia's builder bonus (+15% build speed) makes River Dock spam faster
- Polynesia's naval bonus could extend to river control (future consideration)

6. FLOW DIRECTION VISUALIZATION
River tiles render with a subtle animated flow direction indicator (small chevrons or ripple animation moving in the flow direction). This communicates to the player which way resources will travel without needing to check a tooltip. The direction should be immediately readable at normal game zoom.

7. DATA MODEL
River tile data (stored per tile in map data):
- terrain_type: "river"
- flow_direction: Vector2i (e.g., (1, 0) for flowing east)
- river_id: int (which river system this tile belongs to)
- river_width: int (1 or 2)
- is_navigable: bool (true for all river tiles — ships can use rivers)

River Dock building data (in data/buildings/river_dock.json):
- transport_speed_multiplier: 3.0
- max_barge_capacity: 30 (resources per trip)
- barge_spawn_interval: 5.0 (seconds between barge dispatches)

CONSEQUENCES

- Rivers become the most strategically important terrain feature after mountains
- Early Bronze Age river control provides significant economic advantage
- Creates natural conflict points: the riverbanks near mines
- Adds a raiding dimension: disrupt enemy economy by sinking barges or destroying docks
- River placement in map generation must be balanced relative to starting positions
- The mechanic rewards map reading — experienced players identify good river positions immediately
- Mesopotamia (builders) benefits from cheap fast River Dock construction
- Polynesia (naval) could get river-specific bonuses in future balance passes
- Visual barge movement adds life and activity to the map
- "Resources in transit" creates risk/reward: faster economy but vulnerable to raids

description: Add China as a new playable civilization. China is a technology/economy-focused civ with bonuses to research speed and Knowledge generation. Unique building: Academy (replaces Library, +75% Knowledge gen + unlocks extra tech slot). Unique unit: Chu-Ko-Nu (replaces Archer, rapid-fire crossbow with faster attack speed but lower damage per hit). Unique techs: Papermaking (research cost reduction), Gunpowder Mastery (earlier access to gunpowder-era units). Includes all art assets, data JSON files, civ bonus integration, and tests.
notes: Balance framework: ADR-022 (roots-of-reason-kif). China fills research niche. Anti-dominance: research+economy only. Singularity rush guard: China reaches Singularity victory at most 3 minutes faster than generic civ.

description:
Wire Town Center destruction events to the tech regression engine.

Trigger logic:
When a Town Center building is destroyed:
1. Check the destroyer: must be an enemy player (player_id != owner_id AND player_id != -1/Gaia)
2. Check the building was fully constructed (not a foundation/in-progress)
3. Check knowledge_burning.enabled is true
4. Check cooldown: if cooldown_seconds > 0, verify enough time has passed since this player's last tech regression
5. If all checks pass: call regress_latest_tech(tc_owner_id)
6. If a tech was lost (non-null return):
   - Emit signal: knowledge_burned(attacker_id, defender_id, tech_data)
   - This signal is consumed by the notification system and visual effects

Integration points:
- Hook into the building destruction event handler in the combat system
- The building destruction system already handles HP -> 0 -> destroy. Add a post-destruction check: if building.type == "town_center", evaluate tech regression.
- Must fire AFTER the building is fully destroyed (ruins placed, etc.) but BEFORE the defeat check (so if this was the last TC, the tech loss notification comes before the defeat screen)

Edge case handling:
- Multiple TCs destroyed within cooldown window: only the first triggers regression (if cooldown > 0)
- TC destroyed by its own player (demolish): does NOT trigger regression
- TC destroyed by Gaia (wolf, environmental): does NOT trigger regression
- Age advancement in progress at the destroyed TC: cancel the advancement, refund resources, but this cancellation does NOT count as a tech regression (the regression is a separate event from the TC kill)

Testing:
- Unit test: TC destroyed by enemy triggers regression
- Unit test: TC demolished by self does NOT trigger
- Unit test: TC destroyed with empty research history causes no regression
- Unit test: cooldown prevents double-regression
- Integration test: destroy TC, verify most recent tech is reverted and units lose bonuses

Acceptance criteria:
- TC destruction by enemy triggers exactly 1 tech regression
- Self-destruction and Gaia destruction do not trigger
- Cooldown setting is respected
- Event fires between building destruction and defeat check
- All edge cases handled gracefully
- Signal emitted for UI/notification consumption
title: Town Center destruction triggers tech regression

description:
Implement the dramatic visual and audio feedback when tech regression occurs.

Visual effects at the destroyed TC location:
- "Burning scroll" particle effect: orange/amber paper fragments rising from the TC ruins
- Particle duration: 3 seconds, rising upward and fading
- Small golden glow that intensifies then fades at the ruin site
- Phase 1 placeholder: amber circle expanding from TC position and fading

Screen effects for the affected player:
- Brief amber/red screen flash (0.3 seconds, 20% opacity overlay)
- Screen shake: subtle (2px amplitude, 0.5 seconds)
- These only show for the player who LOST the tech (not the attacker)

Notifications:
- Defending player: large center-screen notification "Knowledge Lost: [Tech Name]" with the tech icon, fades after 4 seconds
- Attacking player: notification "Enemy knowledge destroyed: [Tech Name]" (smaller, in notification feed)
- All other players: event log entry "[Player] lost knowledge of [Tech Name]"
- Notification color: amber/gold (distinct from red damage notifications and green positive notifications)

Audio:
- "Knowledge lost" sound: a deep resonant chime combined with a paper-burning crackle
- Plays for the affected player at high priority (interrupts other game sounds briefly)
- Attacking player hears a softer "triumph" variant

Tech tree UI reaction:
- If the tech tree viewer is open, the lost tech un-highlights with a "burning away" animation
- The tech node briefly glows amber, then reverts to its "available but not researched" state
- If the tech tree is closed, the un-highlight happens silently (will show correctly when opened)

Minimap indicator:
- Brief amber flash at the location of the destroyed TC on the minimap
- Lasts 2 seconds

Chat/event log:
- Timestamped entry: "[12:45] Your city was sacked! Lost: Iron Casting (+2 Infantry Attack)"
- Include what the tech did so the player understands the impact

Acceptance criteria:
- Burning scroll particles visible at destroyed TC
- Screen flash and shake feel impactful but not disorienting
- All three notification tiers work (defender, attacker, observers)
- Audio cue is distinctive and immediately recognizable
- Tech tree UI correctly un-highlights the lost tech
- Event log entry includes tech name and effect description
- All visuals use Phase 1 placeholder approach (simple shapes/colors, no art required)
title: Knowledge burning: visual effects, notifications, and audio

description: Add historically significant technologies missing from the tech tree to improve plausibility and strategic depth. Adds techs spanning Stone Age (Agriculture) through Singularity Age (Brain-Computer Interface), filling gaps in foundational innovations (Wheel, Agriculture), scientific progression (Astronomy, Optics), governance (Democracy), transportation (Aviation), and energy (Renewable Energy). See ADR-021 for full impact analysis and historical rationale.
title: Tech Tree Historical Expansion — 13 new technologies across all ages

description: Add Agriculture as a Stone Age tech requiring Stone Tools. Effects: unlock Farm building (currently implicitly available), +food gather rate. This is the most important tech in human history — the Neolithic Revolution enabled permanent settlement, population growth, and surplus that funded all subsequent specialization. Should be prerequisite for Irrigation. Research time ~35s, cost ~80 Food.
title: Agriculture tech (Stone Age) — foundation of civilization

description: Add Spaceflight as an Information Age tech requiring Rocketry. Effects: prestige bonus, +research speed (Apollo drove miniaturization). Alternative path alongside military Guided Missiles branch. The Space Race was one of history's most significant technological achievements. Cost ~600 Gold, 700 Knowledge, research ~85s.
title: Spaceflight tech (Information Age) — crewed space program

description: Add Renewable Energy as an Information Age tech requiring Electricity + Chemistry. Effects: +sustainable resource generation, alternative to Nuclear Fission path. For a game where the endgame is AGI, the energy question is critical — training AI models requires enormous power. Cost ~500 Gold, 600 Knowledge, research ~75s.
title: Renewable Energy tech (Information Age) — sustainable power

description: Add Brain-Computer Interface as a Singularity Age tech requiring Neural Networks + Antibiotics. Effects: +research speed, alternative Singularity Chain branch. BCI research is a historically emerging parallel path to AGI. Could create an interesting strategic fork: pure software AI (Transformer Architecture) vs neuromorphic approach (BCI). Cost ~1200 Gold, 2000 Knowledge, research ~85s.
title: Brain-Computer Interface tech (Singularity Age) — neuroscience path to AGI

description: Add Trade Routes or Mercantilism as a Medieval-to-Industrial bridge tech requiring Guilds + Compass. Effects: +trade income, unlock trade caravan unit. The Silk Road, maritime trade networks, and global commerce were as transformative as any military tech. Currently trade is underdeveloped in the tree. Cost ~300 Gold, 200 Knowledge, research ~65s.
title: Trade Routes tech (Medieval/Industrial) — global commerce

description: Add The Wheel as a Bronze Age tech requiring Stone Tools. Effects: +movement speed for trade/transport units, prerequisite for later transport techs. Historically (~3500 BCE), the wheel enabled pottery (potter's wheel), transport, and eventually all machinery. Should be ancestral prerequisite for Railroad. Cost ~125 Food, 75 Wood, research ~40s.
title: The Wheel tech (Bronze Age) — transport and machinery foundation

description: Add Road Building as an Iron Age tech requiring Engineering. Effects: +unit movement speed on owned territory, +trade route income. Roman roads were as strategically important as legions. Should be prerequisite for Railroad (more natural chain: Road Building → Railroad). Cost ~200 Stone, 150 Gold, research ~60s.
title: Road Building tech (Iron Age) — territorial movement bonus

description: Add Astronomy as an Iron Age tech requiring Mathematics. Effects: +naval LOS, enables celestial navigation. Should be prerequisite for Compass (instead of just Trireme+Mathematics) and an ancestor of Rocketry. Ancient civilizations navigated by stars for millennia before the compass. Cost ~200 Gold, 150 Knowledge, research ~60s.
title: Astronomy tech (Iron Age) — bridges navigation and science

description: Add Mechanical Power as a Medieval Age tech requiring Engineering. Effects: +25% farm processing, +15% wood gather rate. Wind/water mills transformed grain processing and manufacturing centuries before steam. Should be prerequisite for Steam Power (natural chain: Mechanical Power → Steam Power). Cost ~250 Wood, 150 Gold, research ~65s.
title: Mechanical Power tech (Medieval Age) — wind and water mills

description: Add Optics as a Medieval Age tech requiring Mathematics. Effects: +ranged unit LOS, +10% ranged accuracy. Historically bridges medieval craftsmanship and the scientific revolution. Connects to Semiconductor Fab chain (lithography is optics). Cost ~200 Gold, 150 Knowledge, research ~60s.
title: Optics tech (Medieval Age) — lenses, telescopes, microscopes

description: Add Telegraph as its own Industrial Age tech requiring Electricity (instead of being a building unlock). Effects: +diplomacy speed, enables espionage mechanics, prerequisite for Internet. The telegraph (1837) was the 'Victorian Internet' — the first instant long-distance communication. Should be on the path to Internet rather than just a building. Cost ~300 Gold, 200 Knowledge, research ~65s.
title: Telegraph tech (Industrial Age) — communication revolution

description: Add Aviation as an Industrial Age tech requiring Steam Power + Ballistics. Effects: unlock air reconnaissance unit, +trade range, +LOS. Powered flight (1903) reshaped warfare, trade, and civilization. Currently a major gap — the tree jumps from Rocketry to Satellites with no aircraft. Should be prerequisite for Rocketry. Cost ~400 Gold, 300 Knowledge, research ~75s.
title: Aviation tech (Industrial Age) — powered flight

description: Add Democracy as an Industrial Age tech requiring Code of Laws + Printing Press. Effects: -corruption, +happiness/loyalty, +villager efficiency. Currently the governance chain is thin (Code of Laws → Feudalism → Civil Service). Democracy would give non-military strategic paths more depth and enable future diplomatic victory conditions. Cost ~350 Gold, 250 Knowledge, research ~70s.
title: Democracy tech (Industrial Age) — governance depth

description:
Implement an ordered research history for each player and the engine to revert technologies.

Research history:
- Each player maintains an ordered Array of researched tech IDs (chronological order)
- When a tech is researched, append its ID to the history
- Age advancements are tracked separately (not in the tech history array) — they are immune to regression
- History is saved/loaded as part of game state serialization

Tech regression engine:
- Function: regress_latest_tech(player_id: int) -> Dictionary
  - Pops the last entry from the player's research history
  - Returns the tech data (for notifications/visuals)
  - If history is empty, returns null (no tech to lose)

- Function: revert_tech_effects(player_id: int, tech_id: String) -> void
  - Removes the tech's bonuses from all of the player's units, buildings, and economy
  - For stat bonuses (e.g., +2 attack): subtract from all current units of affected type
  - For economic multipliers (e.g., +15% farm output): recalculate all affected production rates
  - For building unlocks: set the building type as unavailable for new construction (existing buildings remain)
  - For unit unlocks: set the unit type as unavailable for new production (existing units remain)
  - Emit signal: tech_regressed(player_id, tech_id, tech_data)

- Function: can_research_tech(player_id: int, tech_id: String) -> bool
  - Existing function, but must now also return true for previously-researched-then-lost techs
  - A lost tech becomes available for re-research if prerequisites are still met

Tech data model additions:
- Add to each player state: research_history: Array[String] (ordered tech IDs)
- Add to tech data: is_researched_by: Dictionary[int, bool] (player_id -> researched)
- When tech is regressed: is_researched_by[player_id] = false, and remove from history

Data file: data/settings/knowledge_burning.json
{
  "enabled": true,
  "trigger": "town_center_destroyed",
  "tech_loss_count": 1,
  "protect_age_advancements": true,
  "protect_first_n_techs": 0,
  "re_research_cost_multiplier": 1.0,
  "cooldown_seconds": 0
}

Acceptance criteria:
- Research history correctly tracks order of all tech research per player
- regress_latest_tech removes the most recent tech and reverts all its effects
- Unit stat bonuses correctly removed from all existing units
- Economic bonuses correctly recalculated
- Building/unit unlocks correctly reverted (existing instances preserved, new production blocked)
- Lost tech can be re-researched at full cost
- Age advancements are never regressed
- Settings from knowledge_burning.json are respected
- All functions have unit tests with 90%+ coverage
title: Tech research history tracking and regression engine

description:
Define and implement how tech regression interacts with the Singularity win condition chain.

The Singularity path (GPU Foundry → Transformer Architecture → AGI Core) represents the highest-value techs in the game. Losing them to TC destruction should be devastating but recoverable.

Interactions:
1. Transformer Architecture is a researched tech — it CAN be lost via regression if it is the most recently researched tech. If lost:
   - AGI Core cannot be constructed (requires Transformer Architecture)
   - If AGI Core is currently under construction: construction PAUSES (not cancelled, not destroyed — just paused, resumable after re-research)
   - Re-research cost: full (extremely expensive in Knowledge)
   - This can delay a Singularity victory by several minutes of game time

2. GPU Foundry and AGI Core are BUILDINGS, not techs. They are not directly affected by tech regression. However:
   - If the tech that unlocked GPU Foundry is lost, the existing GPU Foundry remains but you cannot build another
   - AGI Core depends on Transformer Architecture — if that tech is lost, AGI Core construction pauses

3. Research order strategy:
   - Savvy players will research Transformer Architecture early in their tech queue, then research other techs after it
   - This pushes Transformer Architecture deeper in the history stack, making it less likely to be the "most recent" tech lost
   - Players who rush Singularity techs last are more vulnerable to regression

4. Defense escalation in late game:
   - When a player researches Transformer Architecture, all opponents receive an alert: "[Player] has researched Transformer Architecture" (public knowledge event, like Wonder construction)
   - This signals that the Singularity push has begun, incentivizing others to target that player's TCs
   - The tech leader must invest heavily in defense to protect their research investment
   - This creates a natural "all vs leader" dynamic in the late game

5. UI indicator for Singularity vulnerability:
   - When a player has Singularity-chain techs, their TC info panel shows a "Knowledge at Risk" warning
   - Lists which Singularity tech would be lost if this TC falls
   - Encourages the player to build defenses

Data considerations:
- Tag Singularity-chain techs in tech data: "singularity_chain": true
- These techs trigger the public alert when researched
- AGI Core construction checks for Transformer Architecture status on every tick (not just on start)

Acceptance criteria:
- Transformer Architecture can be lost via regression, pausing AGI Core construction
- AGI Core construction resumes automatically when Transformer Architecture is re-researched
- Public alert fires when Singularity-chain techs are researched
- Tech research order creates meaningful strategic depth (research Singularity techs early to protect them)
- UI shows "Knowledge at Risk" warning on TCs when Singularity techs are researched
- Integration test: destroy TC, lose Transformer Architecture, verify AGI Core pauses, re-research, verify resume
title: Tech regression interaction with Singularity victory path

description:
Generate AI art for the Town Center building — the most important building in the game. This is the first building players construct and serves as the central hub for resource drop-off and villager production.

## Technical Specs

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Canvas size | 384x256px (3x3 footprint per ADR-008) |
| Tile base | 128x64 diamond per tile, 3x3 = ~384x192 footprint |
| Magenta mask | #FF00FF region for player color recoloring (banner/flag) |
| Naming | `town_center.png` |
| Output path | `assets/sprites/buildings/placeholder/town_center.png` (replaces procedural placeholder) |
| Variant | Generate at 1024x768 or higher, downscale to 384x256 |

## Art Generation Prompt

```
Create a 2D isometric building sprite for a Town Center in an RTS game (Age of Empires style, Stone Age era).

TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS:
- Canvas size 1024x768 pixels (will be downscaled to 384x256)
- Isometric 3/4 top-down view (~30 degree camera angle, 2:1 diamond ratio)
- Transparent background (PNG with alpha)
- Building sits on a 3x3 tile diamond footprint
- Consistent lighting: top-left light source
- No drop shadow (handled by the engine)
- Clean art with defined outlines (visible at small size)
- Include a magenta (#FF00FF) region on the main banner/flag for player color recoloring

BUILDING DESIGN — STONE AGE TOWN CENTER:
- Large communal structure — the heart of a primitive settlement
- Rectangular stone and timber hall with a prominent peaked thatched roof
- Visible from isometric top-down: mostly roof with walls visible on the south and east faces
- Roof: thick golden-brown thatch (#B8964A to #8B7235), slightly uneven/organic
- Walls: rough-cut stone base (#9B9B8B to #787870) with timber frame (#6B5530 to #8B7355)
- Wooden support beams visible at corners and along walls
- Open entrance archway on the south-facing wall (dark interior visible)
- A tall wooden flagpole rises from the roof peak with a magenta (#FF00FF) banner/pennant
- Small fire pit or brazier near the entrance with a warm orange glow
- Wooden crates and pottery near the entrance (supplies/drop-off point visual)
- Flat stone courtyard/platform extending slightly beyond the building footprint

COLOR PALETTE:
- Thatch roof: golden-brown (#B8964A, highlight #D4B87A, shadow #8B7235)
- Stone walls: warm gray (#9B9B8B, highlight #B8B8A8, shadow #787870)
- Timber frame: medium brown (#8B7355, highlight #A08B6B, shadow #6B5530)
- Interior shadow: deep brown-black (#2A2218)
- Flagpole: dark wood (#5C4A3A)
- Flag/banner: magenta (#FF00FF) — player color mask
- Fire glow: warm orange (#E8A040, highlight #FFD080)
- Ground/platform: sandy stone (#C4B896)
- Crates/pottery: earthy brown and terracotta (#A07850, #C47040)

COMPOSITION:
- Building fills roughly 75-80% of the canvas
- Centered horizontally, positioned so the diamond footprint aligns with a 3x3 iso grid
- Roof peak at roughly the upper third of the canvas
- Entrance facing the viewer (south in isometric = toward bottom-right of screen)
- Flag extends above the roofline as the highest point
- Small environmental details (crates, pots) near base add visual interest without clutter

STYLE REFERENCE:
- Age of Empires 2 Town Center (Stone/Dark Age)
- Painterly but clean — not pixel art, not photorealistic
- Warm earth tones, visible at small size, reads clearly as "important building"
```

## Construction State

Also generate a construction-in-progress variant:

```
Same building as above, but at 50% construction:
- Only the stone foundation and lower walls visible (no roof)
- Wooden scaffolding frames around the walls
- Scattered building materials: log piles, stone blocks, rope
- Partial timber frame visible at top where roof will go
- Same flagpole but shorter, no banner yet
- Overall impression: "being built, not finished"
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- Final sprite is 384x256px with transparent background
- Isometric perspective matches terrain tiles (grass_quad.png, dirt_quad.png)
- Visually reads as the most important building at game zoom levels
- Magenta mask region present for player color shader
- Art style consistent with existing tree/terrain/villager sprites
- Construction variant clearly reads as "under construction"
title: Generate Town Center building sprite (Phase 2 art)

description:
At game start, automatically place a Town Center construction site at the player's starting position. Deduct resources and assign all starting villagers to build it.

This avoids forcing the player to manually place their first building before learning the UI. The TC site appears immediately with villagers walking toward it.

Implementation:
- In game_bootstrapper or prototype_main, after spawning villagers:
  1. Call building_placer to place a TC at the starting position (or create construction site directly)
  2. Deduct 275 Wood + 100 Stone via ResourceManager.spend()
  3. Mark footprint cells as solid in pathfinder
  4. Auto-assign all starting villagers to build the site (reuse game_flow_controller.on_building_placed logic)
- The TC appears as a semi-transparent construction site (construction_alpha: 0.4) with progress bar

Acceptance criteria:
- TC construction site appears at game start at the starting position
- Starting resources are deducted (player starts with 0 Wood, 0 Stone after placement)
- All 5 starting villagers auto-walk to the site and begin building
- Construction progress is visible (progress bar, alpha)
- TC completes and becomes functional (drop-off, production queue, garrison)
- Pathfinder cells are marked solid for the TC footprint
title: Auto-place TC construction site at spawn

description:
Update AI economy to handle starting without a pre-built Town Center. AI should build its TC as the very first action.

Current behavior:
- AI starts with a pre-built TC created by _create_ai_town_center()
- AI build order assumes TC already exists for unit production

New behavior:
- AI starts with only villagers (no TC)
- AI auto-places TC construction site at its starting position (mirror player logic)
- All AI starting villagers assigned to build the TC
- AI build order proceeds normally after TC completes
- AI cannot train units or execute build orders until TC is finished

Implementation:
- Reuse the same auto-place logic from story 3 for AI player
- AI economy tick should detect "no TC" state and not attempt production/gathering
- Once TC completes, normal AI behavior resumes

Acceptance criteria:
- AI starts without a TC
- AI places and builds TC as first action
- AI villagers work on TC construction
- AI resumes normal economy/military after TC completes
- AI difficulty multipliers still apply to build speed
title: AI nomadic start — build TC as first priority

description:
GathererComponent currently assumes a drop-off building exists when transitioning to MOVING_TO_DROP_OFF state. With the nomadic start, there may be no drop-off until the TC is built.

Current behavior:
- When carry capacity is full, gatherer scans for nearest drop-off
- If no drop-off found, behavior is undefined (potential null reference or hang)

New behavior:
- If no drop-off exists when villager's carry is full, villager idles with resources held
- Periodically re-check for a drop-off (e.g., every 2-3 seconds)
- Once a drop-off becomes available (TC finishes construction), resume deposit
- If villager is ordered to gather before TC exists, they gather until full then wait

Acceptance criteria:
- No crash or hang when gathering without a drop-off building
- Villager holds resources and waits when no drop-off available
- Villager auto-deposits once a drop-off building completes
- Existing gathering behavior unchanged when drop-off exists
title: Handle no-drop-off gathering edge case

description:
Victory manager registers Town Centers during setup_victory() and uses them for conquest victory detection. With no starting TC, this needs adjustment.

Current behavior:
- setup_victory() iterates buildings looking for completed town_centers
- Conquest victory triggers when all of a player's TCs are destroyed
- No starting TC means the player could be immediately "defeated"

New behavior:
- Victory manager does not check conquest conditions until at least one TC has been built per player
- Under-construction TCs count as "player has TC intent" (not defeated yet)
- Once a player's first TC completes, normal conquest rules apply
- If a player's TC is destroyed before completion (e.g., enemy rush), they lose

Implementation:
- Add a grace period or "has_built_tc" flag per player
- construction_complete signal from buildings should register TCs with victory manager
- Conquest check skips players who haven't completed their first TC yet

Acceptance criteria:
- No immediate defeat at game start (no TC exists yet)
- Under-construction TC prevents conquest defeat
- Completed TC registers with victory manager normally
- Destroying an enemy's only TC (after completion) triggers conquest victory
- Save/load preserves the "has built TC" state
title: Update victory manager for nomadic start

description:
Generate 4 desert tile variants at 1024x1024 using image generation.

Prompt:

Isometric diamond tile for a 2D RTS game. The tile is a perfect diamond (rhombus) shape with a 2:1 width-to-height ratio, viewed from a top-down isometric camera angle (approximately 30 degrees from horizontal). description:
## Context

ADR-004 established 3 launch civilizations (Mesopotamia, Rome, Polynesia) with distinct playstyles: builder, military, naval. The game is expanding to 7 civilizations. New additions must maintain the strategic triangle balance, offer genuinely different gameplay, and not create dominant strategies that invalidate existing civs.

## Decision

### Why These 4 Civilizations

**Selection criteria:** Each new civ must (a) fill an unoccupied strategic niche, (b) have rich historical identity for the Stone→Singularity timeline, (c) not duplicate an existing civ's core bonus category.

| New Civ | Niche | Justification |
|---------|-------|---------------|
| **Egypt** | Wonder/Monument Builder | Stone gathering + building HP bonuses create a defensive/wonder rush alternative to Mesopotamia's speed-focused building. Egypt trades build speed for durability. |
| **China** | Research Accelerator | +20% research speed is the only civ directly bonusing the Singularity win condition. High risk/high reward — faster tech but no military/economy bonus. |
| **Vikings** | Raid/Hybrid | Infantry attack + naval speed creates aggressive early-mid game pressure. Only civ that's strong on both land and water simultaneously. |
| **Maya** | Knowledge Economy | -15% age advancement cost + Knowledge gen creates the fastest age progression. Weak military identity (short-range unique unit) — trades combat for speed. |

### Balance Framework — 7-Civ Matrix

**Core constraint:** No civ should have >55% win rate vs any other civ in equal-skill matchups.

**Playstyle taxonomy (each civ occupies exactly one primary + one secondary):**
- Economy: Mesopotamia (primary), Egypt (secondary)
- Military: Rome (primary), Vikings (secondary)
- Naval: Polynesia (primary), Vikings (secondary)
- Research: China (primary), Maya (secondary)
- Defense: Egypt (primary), Rome (secondary)
- Aggression: Vikings (primary), Polynesia (secondary)

**Balance levers per civ (tunable in JSON, never hardcoded):**
- Passive bonus magnitude (e.g., +15% build speed)
- Unique unit stat delta from base unit (e.g., Berserker HP vs Infantry HP)
- Unique building economic advantage (e.g., Ziggurat's 0.75/s vs Library's 0.5/s)
- Unique tech power level and age availability

**Anti-dominance rules:**
1. No civ gets bonuses in more than 2 categories (e.g., Vikings: military + naval, NOT military + naval + economy)
2. Every unique unit must lose to at least one standard counter unit (e.g., Berserker still loses to massed Archers)
3. Research-boosting civs (China, Maya) must not reduce Singularity victory time by more than 20% vs a generic civ at equal play
4. No unique ability can be strictly better than the base unit it replaces — there must be a tradeoff (e.g., Chu-Ko-Nu: faster fire but lower per-hit damage)

### Unique Ability Safety Analysis

| Ability | Risk | Mitigation |
|---------|------|------------|
| Berserker rage (+50% attack at <30% HP) | Could snowball in large battles | Rage is per-unit, not army-wide. At <30% HP, unit dies in 1-2 more hits. Net DPS increase is ~15% averaged over full combat, not 50%. |
| China +20% research speed | Singularity rush too fast | Research speed bonus is additive with other bonuses, not multiplicative. At Singularity Age (5.0x multiplier), 20% base bonus becomes relatively smaller. Testing target: China Singularity victory at most 3 minutes faster than generic civ. |
| Maya -15% age cost | Age rushing to unlock late techs early | Cost reduction applies to resource cost only, not prerequisite techs. Still must research all prereqs. Testing target: Maya reaches Singularity Age at most 2 minutes faster. |
| Observatory map reveal | Free intel eliminates fog advantage | Radius limited to 15 tiles (vs full map). Does not reveal moving units, only terrain. Opponent can still surprise from outside radius. |
| War Chariot ranged+melee | Overpowered hybrid unit | Lower HP than pure Cavalry, shorter range than Archer. Jack of all trades, master of none. Costs more than either base unit. |

## Consequences

- 7 civs provides enough variety for replayability without ballooning art/test scope beyond feasibility
- Each civ has a clear strategic identity visible from the selection screen
- Balance is tunable via JSON — no code changes needed to adjust civ strength
- Balance audit bead (combat matchup test suite in debug epic) validates anti-dominance rules in CI
- Future civ additions (post-launch) should follow this same framework and pass the same balance checks
title: ADR-022: Civilization Expansion — From 3 to 7 Civilizations

description: Create automated performance benchmarks that detect regressions in CI. Scenarios: (1) Map rendering: 128x128 map, measure frame time with camera pan. (2) Unit pathfinding: 50 units simultaneously, measure completion time. (3) Combat: 100 units in melee, measure frame time. (4) Economy: 50 villagers gathering, measure per-frame cost. description:
Create .github/workflows/ci.yml that runs on every push and PR.

Pipeline stages (in order):
1. Checkout + cache Godot binary
2. Import project (godot --headless --import)
3. Run gdlint (static analysis)
4. Run gdformat --check (formatting)
5. Run GdUnit4 tests with coverage
6. Export builds (macOS + Windows)
7. Upload build artifacts

Use official Godot Linux headless binary. Pin exact Godot version (e.g., 4.3-stable).
Cache .godot/imported/ for faster reimports.
Fail fast on lint/format, don't waste time on tests if code is malformatted.

Acceptance criteria:
- CI passes on clean main branch
- CI fails when a test fails
- CI fails when lint/format fails
- Build artifacts downloadable from Actions UI
title: Set up GitHub Actions CI with Godot headless runner

description:
Generate rivers during procedural map creation. Rivers flow from high elevation to low elevation with encoded flow direction per tile.

Generation algorithm:
1. After terrain elevation is generated, identify watershed sources: tiles at local elevation maxima (mountains, large hills) with elevation > threshold
2. For each source, trace a path downhill following steepest gradient neighbor
3. When two paths meet the same tile, merge into one river (wider)
4. Terminate when reaching ocean, lake, or map edge
5. Assign flow_direction (Vector2i) to each river tile pointing downstream
6. Assign river_id to group tiles belonging to the same river system
7. Set river_width: 1 tile for headwaters/streams, 2 tiles for rivers after a merge or after N tiles from source

River tile data model:
- terrain_type: 'river' (distinct from 'ocean' and 'lake')
- flow_direction: Vector2i — one of 8 directions (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW)
- river_id: int — which river system
- river_width: 1 or 2

Generation constraints:
- Each map (small/medium/large) should have 2-4 rivers
- Rivers should not start or end within 8 tiles of a player starting position (avoid giving one player an unfair river advantage at spawn)
- At least 1 river should pass within 10 tiles of each starting position (everyone gets river access)
- Rivers must be continuous — no gaps in the tile path
- Rivers should not run perfectly straight — add slight noise to the path for natural appearance
- Rivers can flow through forest (clearing a 1-tile path) but not through mountains

Movement rules for river tiles:
- Land units CAN cross river tiles at 50% movement speed (fording)
- Naval units CAN traverse river tiles (rivers are navigable)
- Buildings CANNOT be placed on river tiles (but River Dock can be placed adjacent)

Visualization:
- River tiles use a distinct blue shade (#4A90C4) different from ocean (#2E5B8A) and lake (#5B9BD5)
- Subtle animated flow indicator: small chevrons or ripples moving in flow_direction
- 2-wide rivers render as a wider water channel

Add river tiles to the procedural tile generator (Phase 1 placeholder: light blue diamond with arrow indicating flow direction).

Acceptance criteria:
- Generated maps contain 2-4 rivers with visible flow direction
- Rivers flow continuously from source to terminus
- Flow direction is consistent and always points downstream
- River tiles are traversable by land (slowly) and naval units
- Starting positions have balanced river access
- River data (flow_direction, river_id) accessible to other systems
title: River generation with flow direction and hydrology

description:
Extend the starting location fairness algorithm to account for river access.

When selecting starting locations, the existing fairness system ensures balanced resource access and minimum distance. Add river access as a fairness dimension:

Scoring criteria for starting locations:
- River proximity: distance from starting position to nearest river tile (lower = better)
- River direction: does the nearest river flow TOWARD the starting position? (flow toward = high value, flow away = low value)
- River resource access: how many resource nodes (gold, stone) are within 5 tiles of the river AND within 15 tiles of the starting position? (more = better)

Fairness constraint: the river access score variance across all starting positions must be below a threshold. If one player has a river flowing toward their base past 3 gold mines, other players should have comparable river advantages.

If perfect fairness is not achievable with the generated map, the generator should retry river generation (up to 3 attempts) or adjust river paths slightly to improve balance.

Edge cases:
- Island/archipelago maps: rivers may not exist on small islands. Skip river fairness for water-heavy maps.
- Desert-heavy maps: fewer rivers. Relax fairness thresholds when total river count is low.
- If a map has no rivers (rare), skip this entirely.

Acceptance criteria:
- Starting locations have comparable river access scores
- River direction relative to start position is factored into fairness
- Resource nodes near rivers are counted in fairness assessment
- Generator retries or adjusts if river balance is too unfair
- Works correctly on all map sizes and types
title: Balance river access in starting location selection

description:

- Spawn near forest terrain (within 3 tiles of forest)
- Minimum 12 tiles from any player starting position (wolves shouldn't rush players at game start)
- Minimum 8 tiles between wolf pack spawn points (spread them across the map)
- At least 1 wolf pack should be roughly equidistant between pairs of players (contested wolves)
- Wolves do NOT spawn on water, mountain, or desert tiles — forests and grassland near forests only

Wolf entity initial state:
- Owner: Gaia (neutral faction, player_id = -1)
- Behavior: patrol (wander within 8-tile radius of spawn point)
- Aggro: hostile to lone villagers within 3 tiles
- Domestication_progress: 0.0 (float, 0.0 to 1.0)
- Fed_by: null (player_id of last player to feed)

description:
Generate 4 river tile variants at 1024x1024 using image generation.

Prompt:

Isometric diamond tile for a 2D RTS game. The tile is a perfect diamond (rhombus) shape with a 2:1 width-to-height ratio, viewed from a top-down isometric camera angle (approximately 30 degrees from horizontal). Pixel art style with clean edges. Transparent background outside the diamond. No objects, units, or structures — terrain surface only. The tile should seamlessly connect with identical copies of itself on all four edges.

Flowing river water with gentle current lines and small eddies. Slightly murky with visible flow direction (upper-left to lower-right). Sunlight from the upper-left creating sparkle highlights. Colors: river blues (#4A90C4, #3A80B4, #5AA0D0).

Output: 4 variants at 1024x1024, downscale to 128x64 for final asset. Save to assets/tiles/terrain/river_*.png
title: Generate terrain tile art: river

description:
Beyond the existing scene smoke test (loads every .tscn), add an integration test that actually runs the game loop. This catches crashes, NaN propagation, and cross-system interaction bugs that file-loading alone cannot detect.

Test design:
1. Boot prototype_main.tscn programmatically
2. Advance 30 seconds of game time via direct _process() calls
3. Assert no NaN values in resource stockpiles
4. Assert no orphaned nodes or error logs
5. Save full game state
6. Load game state
7. Advance 30 more seconds
8. Assert game is still in a valid state (no crashes, resources non-negative, unit counts stable)

Acceptance criteria:
- Single test file, runs in <10s headless
- Catches crashes and NaN propagation in the main game loop
- Save/load mid-run is stable
title: Add full game loop integration smoke test
